profile_summary:
Illinois enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 3-seed with a 24-8 record — a résumé that blends genuine elite-level wins with a troubling pattern of late-game collapses. Against ranked opponents, the Illini went an impressive 8-7, with signature victories including a road win over No. 5 Nebraska (78-69), a road demolition of No. 4 Purdue where freshman Keaton Wagler erupted for a career-high 46 points on nine 3-pointers, a neutral-site win over No. 13 Tennessee, and a home statement win over No. 11 Texas Tech. However, the losses are equally revealing: Illinois dropped games to No. 5 UConn (74-61 at Madison Square Garden), No. 3 Michigan (84-70 at home), No. 10 Michigan State in overtime, and were eliminated by No. 23 Wisconsin 91-88 in overtime in the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals — a game in which they led by 15 in the second half. That Wisconsin loss is the defining data point of Illinois' recent form: over their final five games (wins over Oregon and Maryland, losses to Michigan, a win over Oregon, and the Big Ten Tournament exit), the Illini went 3-2, but both losses were gut-punch overtime collapses in which they held substantial second-half leads. The collapse problem is systemic — Illinois also lost at home to Nebraska in December from ahead, blew a lead at Michigan State, and surrendered overtime losses to both Wisconsin and UCLA, the latter coming after building a 23-point first-half advantage. Illinois plays a high-octane, multi-dimensional offensive style that consistently scores in the 80s and beyond, leads the Big Ten in 3-point shooting, and owns a dominant rebounding edge — but this team can be porous late in games when opposing offenses heat up.

The Illini boast genuine star power: Wagler is one of the most explosive freshmen in the country, scoring in double figures in 21 straight games and capable of single-handedly winning games (see: Purdue); Andrej Stojakovic provides a reliable secondary scoring punch at 17-plus points when healthy; David Mirkovic is a double-double threat from the frontcourt; and the twin Ivisic brothers (Tomislav and Zvonimir) give Illinois a physical interior presence few teams can match. Senior guard Kylan Boswell returned from a broken right hand in late February and has been limited — his defensive impact and playmaking will be critical in March. The Illini are 14-3 at home, 8-5 on the road, and 3-3 on neutral courts, suggesting they are a different team outside the State Farm Center; their neutral-site résumé includes the UConn loss, the Tennessee win, and the Big Ten Tournament overtime exit — a mixed bag. Illinois is confident and explosive when rolling, capable of blowing teams off the floor early, but they are dangerously prone to second-half disengagement and have repeatedly failed to close out games they had in hand. The bottom line: Illinois has the offensive firepower, roster depth, and big-game experience to reach the Elite Eight, and Wagler's ceiling alone makes them a threat to go further — but their chronic late-game collapses and inability to put away elite opponents makes a Final Four run feel like a best-case scenario rather than an expectation. Realistic tournament ceiling: **Elite Eight**, with a first or second-round exit always in play if the second-half fade shows up at the wrong moment.

2025-11-04 - Gonzaga: 98 vs Texas Southern: 43

SPOKANE, Wash. -- — Tyon Grant-Foster scored 15 points and Graham Ike added 13 points and 11 rebounds, as No. 21 Gonzaga opened the season with a 98-43 victory over Texas Southern 98-43 on Monday night. Braden Huff added 14 points on 6-for-8 shooting to help the Bulldogs win their 22nd consecutive regular-season opener and their 36th straight home opener. Jaylen Wysinger and Duane Posey scored eight points apiece to top the Tigers, who never led after going up 10-8 early on a three-point play by Cameron Patterson. The Bulldogs struggled from beyond the arc early, shooting just 3 for 14 from 3-point range in the first half. Gonzaga stayed afloat at the free-throw line (13 for 15) and on the glass, turning 12 offensive rebounds into 17 second-chance points before halftime. Coach Mark Few’s team found its rhythm in the closing minutes of the half, going on a pair of 9-0 runs. Huff sparked the surge with a coast-to-coast finish before dropping in a floater to help the Bulldogs outscore the Tigers 20-5 over the final six minutes of the period. Gonzaga kept the momentum rolling out of halftime. Ike opened the second half with a baseline spin into a two-handed dunk, followed by Huff's right-hook to stretch the lead to 50-27. The Bulldogs expanded their lead to 82-37 later in the half off back-to-back 3-pointers from Adam Miller, who finished with 13 points, including three triples in his Gonzaga debut. Mario Saint-Supery and Jalen Worley also shined in their first outings with the Bulldogs, scoring eight points each. Davis Fogle added 11 points for Gonzaga in his first collegiate game. The Bulldogs won the rebound battle 56-27. Their bench was also more productive, outscoring Texas Southern’s second unit 48-21. Up Next Texas Southern visits Texas A&M on Thursday. 2026-02-26 - Gonzaga: 89 vs Portland: 48

SPOKANE, Wash. -- — Graham Ike scored 19 points and matched a school record with seven steals as No. 9 Gonzaga routed Portland 89-48 on Wednesday night to clinch its first West Coast Conference regular-season championship in three years. Braeden Smith added 15 points for the Bulldogs (28-2, 16-1), who avenged their 87-80 loss at Portland on Feb. 4. Mario Saint-Supery and Tyon Grant-Foster each scored 12, and Ike grabbed eight rebounds. Joel Foxwell led the Pilots (12-18, 5-12) with 12 points. Portland shot 34.7% from the field and committed 22 turnovers. In their final WCC home game before joining the reconstructed Pac-12 next season, the Bulldogs clinched the top seed in the conference tournament for the first time since 2022. Gonzaga has earned the No. 1 seed 11 times in the 17 seasons the WCC Tournament has been held in Las Vegas since 2009. Battling through double teams in the post, Ike stepped outside and drilled a pair of early 3-pointers and accounted for 12 of Gonzaga’s first 17 points. His second 3 sparked a 16-0 run that gave the Bulldogs a double-digit lead. Late in the first half, Saint-Supery tossed a lob to Grant-Foster for a two-handed dunk, which sent Gonzaga into the break with a 36-21 lead. The Bulldogs opened the second half with a 10-0 run — including 3-pointers from Adam Miller and Emmanuel Innocenti — and led by at least 20 the rest of the way. With the game out of reach, coach Mark Few pulled Ike with five minutes remaining. That snapped Ike’s 10-game streak of scoring 20 or more points — the longest such run by a Gonzaga player under Few. The fifth-year forward entered the night leading active Division I players with 2,456 career points. He is the only active Division I player with at least 2,000 points and 1,000 rebounds. Up next Gonzaga visits Saint Mary’s on Saturday looking to secure an outright WCC crown. Portland hosts San Diego on Saturday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here. AP college basketball: and

Joshua Dent scored 14 points and Mantas Juenaz added 12 as the Gaels (27-4, 16-2 WCC) rallied to their seventh consecutive win and 26th straight at home. It's the fourth straight year Saint Mary's has won at least a share of the conference title. Graham Ike had 17 points for Gonzaga (28-3, 16-2) before fouling out with four minutes left. Adam Miller scored 14 and Tyon Grant-Foster added 13 for the Bulldogs, who lost for the second time this season to an unranked opponent. It was the final regular-season game between the long-time WCC rivals before Gonzaga joins the Pac-12 next season. The schools have been the most dominant teams in the conference for the past two-plus decades, combining to win or share the conference title for the past 27 seasons. And this one was as thrilling as so many other games between the schools. Gonzaga had already clinched the top seed for the conference tournament, but the Gaels didn’t go quietly. After trailing by seven at halftime, Saint Mary’s tied it with 11 minutes left — and then pulled away. Lewis made three 3-pointers and had 11 points as part of a 23-6 run. The Bulldogs missed seven of their first nine shots and went through a three-minute scoreless stretch while the Gaels stayed close. Grant-Foster helped the Zags get back on track with six points in three minutes. 2026-03-11 - Gonzaga: 79 vs Santa Clara: 68

LAS VEGAS -- — Gonzaga has dominated the West Coast Conference since coach Mark Few took over at the turn of the century. The Bulldogs are leaving for the Pac-12 Conference next season and made sure to go out winners. Mario Saint-Supéry scored 21 points, Tyon Grant-Foster had 20 and No. 12 Gonzaga defeated Santa Clara 79-68 on Tuesday night to win the WCC Tournament. “It's a special feeling to go out the right way,” said Gonzaga's Graham Ike, voted the tournament Most Outstanding Player. “Ultimately, we ended where we started this. It started out with championships and those teams that came before us. We just wanted to continue the success that they had. We were proud we could do that.” The Bulldogs (30-3) received the conference's automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament, though the only mystery was what seed they will get. This was their sixth WCC tournament title in seven years and 12th in the last 14. Santa Clara (26-8) will wait until Sunday to see if it is in the 68-team field, but the WCC appears to be a three-bid league. Gonzaga, Santa Clara and No. 21 Saint Mary's were in the top 38 of the NCAA's NET rankings entering this game and in the top 35 in Kenpom. Four players scored in double figures for the Zags, who made 52.8% of their shots. Ike made all seven of his shots in scoring 15 points. Christian Hammond led the Broncos with 24 points, and Allen Graves scored 11. Sash Gavalyugov, who scored 23 points that included the dagger 3-pointer in the closing seconds to beat Saint Mary's 76-71 in the semifinals, was held to eight. He was 3 of 13, including 2 of 9 from 3-point range. “Sash had some tough shots tonight,” Santa Clara coach Herb Sendek said. “He does have the ability to make some of those shots, but I don't know that things were engineered to simply take him out.” The Broncos made Gonzaga earn this victory. They led 52-50 nearly midway through the second half before the Bulldogs scored seven straight points to go ahead for good. After dominating the WCC, Gonzaga will see if it can continue to assert itself against San Diego State, Boise State and Utah State in the revamped Pac-12 beginning next season. That will come later. Gonzaga has yet another NCAA Tournament appearance to place its focus. “After like 2015, we really focused on NCAA Tournament wins and Final Fours and things like that,” Few said. “The national championship's going to be the standard that we're aiming for. But we've always talked about taking care of business along the way so that you can get to the prize at the end, which is the NCAA Tournament. These guys have done a good job of keeping their eyes on the prize.” ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). profile_summary:
Gonzaga enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 3-seed with a 30-3 record and the WCC's automatic bid in hand, boasting one of the most complete résumés in program history against non-conference opponents — though two puzzling losses to unranked Portland and Saint Mary's late in the year introduce real questions about ceiling. Against ranked opponents, the Bulldogs went an impressive 6-3: wins over then-No. 23 Creighton (90-63), then-No. 8 Alabama (95-85), then-No. 18 Kentucky (94-59), then-No. 25 UCLA (82-72), then-No. 21 Saint Mary's (73-65 in conference play), and Oregon (though unranked at game time, a quality road neutral win), with losses coming against then-No. 7 Michigan (101-61, a 40-point beatdown in Las Vegas), unranked Portland (87-80 on the road), and then-unranked Saint Mary's (70-59 in Moraga) — the Michigan blowout in particular serves as a cautionary data point about how this team responds when facing a genuinely elite program. Over the final five games with available detail — wins over Washington State (83-53), Santa Clara (94-86), San Francisco (80-59), Portland (89-48 revenge blowout), and a regular-season-closing loss at Saint Mary's (59-70) before winning the WCC Tournament over Oregon State (65-56) and Santa Clara (79-68) — Gonzaga's form is best described as strong but not dominant, with the WCC tournament title providing a timely confidence boost heading into March.

Graham Ike is the engine driving everything, arriving at the tournament as arguably the most dominant big man in the country not named on a top-two seed: he scored 20 or more points in 10 consecutive games before being pulled early against Portland in the regular-season finale, owns 45 career double-doubles, and was named WCC Tournament Most Outstanding Player after going a perfect 7-of-7 from the floor for 15 points in the championship win over Santa Clara — the fifth-year forward is the only active Division I player with 2,000 career points and 1,000 rebounds, and when he is healthy and aggressive, Gonzaga's offense operates at a genuinely elite level through the paint, where they lead the nation at 45.2 points per game in the lane. Braden Huff, the Zags' second offensive pillar averaging 17.8 points per game, missed extended time with a knee injury in late January and remains a question mark in terms of full functionality — his availability and health will be one of the most critical variables in how far this team can advance. Supporting pieces like Mario Saint-Supéry (21 points in the WCC title game), Tyon Grant-Foster, Adam Miller, Emmanuel Innocenti, and Davis Fogle give Gonzaga genuine depth, and coach Mark Few's squad plays with poise and composure in neutral-site environments — their 7-1 record on neutral courts (the lone blemish being that Michigan demolition) is a meaningful indicator for tournament play. The Michigan loss, however, cannot be ignored: when pressed by a team with elite athleticism, length, and defensive pressure, Gonzaga shot 33.8% from the floor and 13.6% from three, and Huff fouled out — a blueprint any elite tournament opponent will have studied. This team is a legitimate Elite Eight threat and capable of a Final Four run if Ike remains dominant and Huff is healthy, but the ceiling appears to stop short of a national title given their demonstrated vulnerability against truly elite defensive pressure and their two late-season stumbles against inferior competition.

Simeon Cottle scored 20 to lead the Owls (8-4), who outrebounded the Tide 46-41 but were minus-eight in turnover margin. RJ Johnson added 16 points off bench. profile_summary: Kennesaw State enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 14-seed with a 21-13 record — a résumé built largely on Conference USA competition with a few revealing data points against tougher opponents. The Owls went 0-2 against ranked foes this season, falling to #16 Alabama 92-81 in December and losing to South Florida early in the year, though they did manage to outrebound Alabama 46-41 and cut a 26-point deficit significantly, closing the second half on a 53-38 run. Against quality mid-major competition they were inconsistent: road losses to Sam Houston, Louisiana Tech, Liberty, and UTEP demonstrate real vulnerability away from home, while home wins over Western Kentucky, New Mexico State, and Missouri State show they can be formidable at Kennesaw. The five-game stretch entering the tournament tells a complicated story — back-to-back road losses at UTEP (78-71) and to New Mexico State (79-76) to close the regular season suggested a team in freefall, but Kennesaw State then reeled off three straight wins in the Conference USA Tournament, beating Western Kentucky, Sam Houston, and Louisiana Tech by margins of 9, 6, and 11 points respectively, earning the automatic bid with genuine momentum. Simeon Cottle, RJ Johnson, Braedan Lue, and Frankquon Sherman give this team four legitimate scoring threats — Johnson erupted for 32 in a loss at Sam Houston and 29 in a win at Missouri State, Cottle delivered a heroic 33-point overtime performance against FGCU including a perfect 14-of-14 from the line, and Sherman has emerged as a versatile contributor, posting 19 points and 10 rebounds in the conference tournament opener. The Owls play an up-tempo, high-scoring style that can produce eye-popping outputs — they've cracked 90 points multiple times — but they are prone to defensive lapses that keep opponents within striking distance or allow second-half runs to bury them, as seen in the UTEP loss where a 9-0 opponent run broke a tied game open. Their home/neutral-site record is notably stronger than their road record, and the conference tournament — played at neutral-site Propst Arena in Huntsville, which the team was specifically noted to have familiarity with — appears to have been a stabilizing confidence builder. Entering the bracket scrappy, hot off three straight wins, and with multiple players capable of putting up 25+ on any given night, Kennesaw State is a credible first-round threat to cause an upset, but their ceiling is realistically a first-round upset win at best — their inability to sustain defensive intensity, a 0-2 record against ranked opponents with no victories over Power Four competition, and an overall profile that feasted on weak non-conference schedules makes a second-round appearance highly unlikely.

EAST LANSING, Mich. -- — Freshman Cam Ward had 18 points and 10 rebounds, Coen Carr scored 15 points and had seven rebounds and No. 22 Michigan State beat No. 14 Arkansas 69-66 on Saturday night in a matchup of Hall of Fame coaches. The Spartans (2-0) grabbed 19 offensive rebounds and converted them into 18 points, a staple of Tom Izzo's program for 31 seasons. John Calipari has highly touted freshmen Darius Acuff and Meleek Thomas in his second season with the Razorbacks (1-1), as he often did at Kentucky, Memphis and Massachusetts. Acuff, who is from Detroit, had 16 points on 5 of 16 shooting points and six assists. Thomas, who is from Pittsburgh, scored 16 on 5 of 15 shooting. Arkansas went on an 8-0 run to pull into a 66-all tie with 1:41 left. Carson Cooper ended Michigan State's scoring drought with a go-ahead free throw and grabbed a defensive rebound on the ensuing possession. profile_summary: Michigan State enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 3-seed with a 25-7 record — a résumé built on genuine toughness against elite competition but also punctuated by frustrating inconsistency down the stretch. The Spartans went an impressive 8-7 against ranked opponents this season, with marquee wins over #14 Arkansas, #12 Kentucky, #16 North Carolina, #24 USC, #5 Illinois, and #8 Purdue, while absorbing losses to #4 Duke (66-60 at home), #13 Nebraska (58-56 on the road), #3 Michigan twice, #10 Minnesota, and #10 Wisconsin — a body of work that proves they can compete at the highest level but haven't been able to consistently close out elite programs. Their final five games tell a mixed story: a 91-87 home escape over Rutgers, a 90-80 loss at #3 Michigan in the regular season finale, and then a 88-84 Big Ten Tournament quarterfinal exit at the hands of UCLA — a team Michigan State had already beaten in the regular season — leaving them on a two-game losing skid entering March Madness. Tom Izzo's team plays a characteristically physical, rebounding-first brand of basketball — they consistently dominate the glass (outrebounding opponents by wide margins in wins over Arkansas, Kentucky, Toledo, and Maryland), generate significant second-chance points, and run an effective transition offense led by the electric Coen Carr, whose highlight-reel dunks and relentless energy set the tone. The identity of this team runs through point guard Jeremy Fears Jr., who has been sensational for stretches — dropping 29 points in overtime at Rutgers, 31 against Michigan, and 26 with 15 assists against Illinois — but his season has also been clouded by repeated sportsmanship concerns, including a technical foul in the Michigan finale for an apparent kick toward Elliot Cadeau, building on similar incidents throughout conference play that raise real questions about his composure in high-stakes moments. Senior center Jaxon Kohler has been quietly outstanding, posting 12 double-doubles and providing a reliable interior anchor, while Carson Cooper stepped up as a force in the second half of the season; the health and availability of Divine Ugochukwu, who hobbled off with an injury against Minnesota in early February, is worth monitoring as a potential depth concern in the backcourt. Michigan State is notably a better team at home (14-2 in East Lansing) and has shown it can win on neutral sites — going 3-1 in neutral-site games including wins over Arkansas, Kentucky, and North Carolina early in the season — but road performances have been shakier, with the Nebraska and Minnesota losses both coming away from home when the Spartans turned the ball over excessively. This is a resilient, battle-tested program under a Hall of Fame coach with Final Four pedigree, but the two-game losing streak, Fears' recurring discipline issues, and an inability to beat Michigan or Nebraska all season are legitimate red flags. **The realistic ceiling for this Michigan State team is the Sweet 16**: experienced and physical enough to grind through a first-round matchup and potentially pull off an upset in the second round, but too inconsistently sharp from three-point range, too prone to turnover-heavy collapses in hostile environments, and too exposed against elite, composed teams to be trusted for a deep Elite Eight or Final Four run.

profile_summary: North Dakota State enters the 2026 NCAA tournament as a 14-seed with a 27-7 record, a remarkable total win count for a mid-major, though their résumé against quality competition carries notable blemishes. Early in the season, the Bison dropped games to Oregon State (67-65 on a buzzer layup), UC Davis (80-68), Arkansas State (85-80), UC Irvine (74-73 in a one-point heartbreaker), and UTEP (76-66) — losses that reveal real vulnerability against mid-major and low-major Power programs outside the Summit League bubble. Their conference dominance is undeniable, however, finishing 12-1 in Summit League play and riding a nine-game winning streak in January and February before Denver snapped it 78-71 on February 6th. Heading into the tournament, the Bison are scalding hot: their final five games produced wins of 95-59 over Kansas City, 96-63 over North Dakota, 76-65 over Oral Roberts, 74-50 over Omaha, and 70-62 over North Dakota in the conference tournament — all comfortable margins that suggest a team playing its best basketball when it matters most. No ranked opponents appear among the game summaries, meaning the Bison have zero wins against nationally ranked competition, which is the central question mark on their entire profile. Their style of play is multi-dimensional and occasionally spectacular: they can win shootouts (99-94 over Drake, with Trevian Carson dropping 29 and Andy Stefonowicz shooting 6-of-7 from three), gut out clutch moments (Damari Wheeler-Thomas' buzzer layup at Omaha after trailing by 12 at halftime), and dismantle opponents in blowouts (111-38 over St. Scholastica, 101-67 over Minnesota Crookston). Key contributors are performing at a high level entering the tournament — Wheeler-Thomas (23 points at Montana, the buzzer winner at Omaha, 23 points at South Dakota State), Carson (29-point explosion at Drake, consistent double-figure scorer throughout), Tay Smith (a remarkable 8-of-10 from three in an 86-58 demolition of Oral Roberts), Treyson Anderson (22 points and three blocks in the comeback at Omaha), and Markhi Strickland providing reliable scoring depth — giving this team genuine multi-threat offensive firepower with no obvious injury concerns surfacing in the summaries. The Bison are 6-5 in true road and neutral-site games when facing teams of substance, and while they showed they can blow leads and fall behind (trailing by 12 at halftime at Omaha, surrendering a nine-point second-half margin against Drake), they also demonstrated the resilience to come back and win both of those games, suggesting a composed, mentally tough group rather than a collapse-prone one. The collapse losses — UTEP, UC Irvine, UC Davis — do indicate they can be susceptible to pressure from faster, more athletic mid-major or mid-level competition, precisely the kind of opponent a 3-seed might represent. **Verdict**: North Dakota State is a dangerous but limited 14-seed — credibly capable of pulling a first-round upset if their offense is clicking, particularly given their recent form and multi-dimensional scoring, but their complete lack of wins against ranked or elite competition and a 6-5 record outside the Summit League make a second-round run highly improbable. Their realistic tournament ceiling is **a first-round upset**, with a likely exit no later than the Round of 32.

The Cornhuskers shot 63% in the second half and scored 56 points after leading just 30-25 at halftime. The Cornhuskers led 52-33 with 11 minutes remaining before a 25-6 run put the game well in hand. Shelton Williams-Dryden scored all six points for the Wolves in that stretch of nearly six minutes. Reink Mast had 10 points and 11 rebounds for the Cornhuskers in their season opener. Jamarques Lawrence scored 18 points and Connor Essegian added 12. Williams-Dryden scored 14 points and Chas Lewless had 11 for the Wolves. West Georgia shot 29% from the field. profile_summary: Nebraska enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 4-seed with a 26-6 record — a résumé built on one of the most remarkable early-season runs in program history but clouded by a late-season swoon that raises legitimate questions about their ceiling. The Cornhuskers raced out to a 20-0 start, the best in program history and the longest active win streak in Division I at 24 games, before the wheels began to come off against elite competition. Against ranked opponents, Nebraska finished approximately 7-6: notable wins over then-No. 9 Michigan State (58-56, on a Rienk Mast tiebreaking 3-pointer), then-No. 13 Illinois on a buzzer-beater (83-80, on Jamarques Lawrence's last-second triple), and a road win at then-No. 5 Indiana after trailing by 16 — but losses to No. 3 Michigan, No. 9 Illinois (at home), No. 13 Purdue (in overtime), No. 9 Iowa, and a blowout at UCLA, plus a 74-58 thumping by Purdue in the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals, reveal a team that can steal a marquee win but hasn't consistently handled elite opponents, especially in the second half of the season. Their style of play is built around perimeter shooting — Nebraska leads the Big Ten in 3-pointers made — and they have demonstrated a remarkable capacity for comebacks, erasing deficits of 16 points multiple times, but they are equally collapse-prone, as evidenced by blowing 10-point leads late in regulation against both Purdue and Iowa. Pryce Sandfort is their engine, a transfer from Iowa who set Nebraska's single-season 3-pointer record and posted multiple 20-plus-point games including a 32-point effort at USC; Rienk Mast (6-10, capable of seven 3-pointers in a game) is a matchup nightmare at the 5; and Jamarques Lawrence and Braden Frager provide punch off the bench and in the starting lineup. However, health is a legitimate concern — Frager missed two games with a sprained ankle, Mast sat out the Michigan loss with illness, and Nebraska's depth was noticeably strained down the stretch. The final five games (Penn State win, Maryland win, USC win, UCLA loss by 20, Iowa overtime win in the regular-season finale) paint a picture of an inconsistent team that can dominate inferior opposition but struggles to maintain focus and execution against disciplined, physical teams — and the 74-58 conference tournament loss to Purdue, where Nebraska was outrebounded by eight and outscored in the paint, is an especially worrying exit. Location matters for this group: they are dominant at Pinnacle Bank Arena but went just 7-3 on the road in Big Ten play, and their neutral-site record is thin beyond early-season tournaments. Nebraska's realistic tournament ceiling is the Sweet 16: they have the star power, shooting, and demonstrated clutch ability to knock off a 5-seed and pull a signature upset, but their late-season inconsistency, vulnerability to physical teams that crash the glass, and second-half offensive droughts make a deeper run unlikely unless Sandfort and Mast are simultaneously locked in — a combination that has been rare since January.

Troy, coming off a 108-107 double-overtime win at San Diego State on Tuesday, led throughout the third overtime but threw the ball away into the front court after rebounding Baker-Mazara's missed drive with about 10 seconds to go. Rodney Rice got the loose ball and fired it ahead to Marsh, who took a couple of dribbles and hit from almost straightaway. profile_summary:
Troy enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 13-seed with a 22-11 record, a résumé that reflects a genuinely competitive mid-major program capable of beating quality opponents but one that has shown persistent vulnerability against elite competition. The Trojans went 1-5 against power-conference foes early in the season — falling to Loyola Marymount, Cal State Northridge, and Toledo — but did knock off UAB on the road and pushed USC and San Diego State to multiple overtimes in November, which speaks to their competitive toughness even in defeats. In conference play, Troy finished as the Sun Belt's top seed, winning the Sun Belt Tournament with semifinal and final victories over Southern Miss (78-70) and Georgia Southern (77-61), capping a strong late-season run that provides genuine momentum heading into March. Their final five games show a mixed but ultimately positive picture: losses to Southern Miss (65-69) and South Alabama (54-65) sandwiched around a dominant 78-59 home win over Louisiana before back-to-back Sun Belt Tournament wins, suggesting a team that stumbled in late February but has genuinely righted the ship at the right moment.

Stylistically, Troy is a resilient, comeback-prone team with demonstrated star power and a flair for the dramatic — qualities that can be both thrilling and alarming in tournament play. Thomas Dowd is the engine of this team, a physical double-double threat who hit a buzzer-beater at UAB, scored 24 points in a revenge win over Arkansas State, and consistently protects the rim; Victor Valdes provides secondary creation as a shot-maker and facilitator; Cooper Campbell is a dangerous perimeter scorer capable of career-high performances (32 points vs. USC in triple overtime); and Cobi Campbell and Theo Seng offer reliable depth scoring. The Trojans have repeatedly demonstrated the ability to force and win overtime games — including a triple-OT near-miss at USC and a double-OT road win at Old Dominion — showing genuine mental fortitude, but they also collapsed against West Georgia in a non-conference overtime loss and dropped games to Southern Miss twice during the regular season, revealing a troubling inconsistency that emerges without warning. Location matters for this group: Troy is notably stronger at home (where they reeled off long Sun Belt winning streaks) and has struggled in true road environments, though their neutral-site tournament performance has been sharp. As a 13-seed, Troy is a classic dangerous upset pick in the first round — battle-tested, multi-dimensional, and peaking at the right time — but their inability to consistently beat elite competition and their mid-season defensive lapses make a run beyond the second round a long shot. **Realistic ceiling: Round of 32**, with an upset win in Game 1 being a genuine possibility.

name: Arkansas
summaries:
2025-11-09 - Michigan State: 69 vs Arkansas: 66

EAST LANSING, Mich. -- — Freshman Cam Ward had 18 points and 10 rebounds, Coen Carr scored 15 points and had seven rebounds and No. 22 Michigan State beat No. 14 Arkansas 69-66 on Saturday night in a matchup of Hall of Fame coaches. The Spartans (2-0) grabbed 19 offensive rebounds and converted them into 18 points, a staple of Tom Izzo's program for 31 seasons. John Calipari has highly touted freshmen Darius Acuff and Meleek Thomas in his second season with the Razorbacks (1-1), as he often did at Kentucky, Memphis and Massachusetts. Acuff, who is from Detroit, had 16 points on 5 of 16 shooting points and six assists. Thomas, who is from Pittsburgh, scored 16 on 5 of 15 shooting. Arkansas went on an 8-0 run to pull into a 66-all tie with 1:41 left. Carson Cooper ended Michigan State's scoring drought with a go-ahead free throw and grabbed a defensive rebound on the ensuing possession. After a scoreless first half with two fouls, the 6-foot-11 senior had nine points in the second half. Jeremy Fears took advantage of Acuff's inexperience, drawing a foul on a 3-point shot with 43.2 seconds left and making two free throws for a three-point lead. Thomas missed a 3-pointer with 27 seconds to go and after Michigan State freshman Jordan Scott missed two free throws, Acuff missed a 3-pointer with seven seconds left. The Razorbacks retained possession and after Izzo called timeout, Trevon Brazile shot an air ball on a 3-pointer. D.J. Wagner scored eight of his 13 points in the first half, helping Arkansas lead 39-36 in the closely contested game. Up next Arkansas: Hosts Central Kansas on Tuesday. Michigan State: Hosts San Jose State on Thursday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-12 - Arkansas: 93 vs Central Arkansas: 56

FAYETTEVILLE, Ark. -- — Darius Acuff Jr. scored 21 points, leading four Arkansas players in double figures, and the No. 21 Razorbacks beat Central Arkansas 93-56 on Tuesday night. Karter Knox added 19 points and 10 rebounds. Meleek Thomas had 17 points and Malique Ewin scored 10. Acuff made 3-pointers on three straight possessions and Knox added a three-point play to stretch Arkansas’ (2-1) lead from 10 to 20 during a 12-2 run early in the second that ultimately ended the Bears’ comeback chances. Another 13-2 run later in the half turned things in a blowout. Arkansas’ defense limited Central Arkansas (1-2), especially in the second half. The Bears shot just 28% from the field and 25% in the final 20 minutes, including a 0-for-18 mark from 3-point range. The Razorbacks held Central Arkansas without a field goal for almost seven minutes during the Razorbacks’ final run. Knox, Arkansas’ second leading returning scorer from last season, scored his first points of the season after missing the opener with a toe injury and playing only 19 minutes against Michigan State. Acuff, a freshman, made 9 of his 16 attempts from the field against Central Arkansas and has led the team in scoring in each of the first three games. Trevon Brazile, who scored 28 points in Arkansas’ season-opening win against Southern, did not play because of back spasms. Arkansas moved to 10-0 all-time against its in-state foe in just the teams’ fourth meeting since 1947. Up Next Arkansas hosts Samford on Friday. Central Arkansas hosts Samford on Sunday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-19 - Arkansas: 84 vs Winthrop: 83

FAYETTEVILLE, Ark. -- — Meleek Thomas scored a career-high 26 points as No. 21 Arkansas scored the final six points to rally past Winthrop 84-83 on Tuesday night. Arkansas took their final lead with 11 seconds left when Nick Pringle scored at the rim after a Winthrop turnover. After the basket, he and Winthrop’s Logan Duncomb became tangled. As the officials attempted to break up the entanglement, Pringle threw Duncomb to the floor, leading to a brief delay as the play went to review. Pringle and Duncomb were issued offsetting technical fouls and four players — two from Arkansas and two from Winthrop — were ejected for coming on to the floor from the bench. Duncomb’s technical resulted in him fouling out, as well. Daylen Berry attempted a potential game-winning 3-pointer from the wing as time expired, but Thomas played tight defense, forcing an awkward attempt and the ball bounced off the rim. Thomas shot 10 for 20 from the field, Trevon Brazile scored 14 points, Pringle 13 and Darius Acuff Jr. added 12 for Arkansas. Winthrop stuck around and carried the lead into the waning seconds thanks to 15-for-31 shooting from 3-point range. Kareem Rozier led the Eagles with 23 points on 8-for-11 shooting from the field, including a 7 for 10 on 3s. Kody Clouet added 16 points, 12 of which came from beyond the arc. Neither team led by more than eight points. UP NEXT Arkansas hosts Jackson State on Friday. Winthrop plays at Jackson State on Sunday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here. AP college basketball: and

Ngongba's basket gave the Blue Devils a 66-64 lead with 5:20 remaining. Foster made a big 3 with 2:31 to go. Cameron Boozer began to assert himself in the final minutes of the first half. He made two 3-pointers, converted a driving layup and picked up an assist on Dame Sarr's dunk during a 10-0 run that gave Duke a 32-22 lead with 5:11 left. The Blue Devils had a 41-35 lead at the break after the Razorbacks closed the first half with a 7-0 spurt. Cameron Boozer had 15 points at halftime on 6-for-9 shooting. 2026-03-01 - Florida: 111 vs Arkansas: 77

GAINESVILLE, Fla. -- — Thomas Haugh scored 22 points, Rueben Chinyelu had his 17th double-double of the season and No. 7 Florida earned at least a share of the Southeastern Conference title with a 111-77 romp over 20th-ranked Arkansas on Saturday night. It tied the worst loss of Arkansas coach John Calipari's career, a 34-pointer against Duke in 2018. Calipari was with Kentucky at the time. The Gators (23-6, 14-2 SEC) closed out an unbeaten February with their ninth consecutive win and locked up their first regular-season championship since 2014. They remained two games ahead of No. 17 Alabama with two to play and can wrap up the outright title Tuesday night at home. But they didn’t wait to celebrate. With a sold-out crowd and ESPN’s College GameDay on hand, Florida turned Billy Donovan Court into a party spot. Coach Todd Golden was front and center as the team doled out hats and T-shirts and cut down nets. It wasn't Golden's first time in the spotlight Saturday night, either. Golden and Calipari got double technicals early in the second half after screaming at each other near midcourt. Calipari’s assistants pulled him away. Golden turned and riled up the 11,000-plus in attendance. It was one of the few highlights in a game that was pretty much over early. The Gators dominated on both ends, making plays inside and out, and built a 30-point lead with 13 minutes to play. The biggest surprises were bench players Urban Klavzar and Isaiah Brown. Klavzar hit his first four 3-pointers and finished with 14 points, and Brown scored 11 thanks partly to two treys. But it was Florida’s big men who controlled the tempo most of the way. Haugh, Chinyelu and Alex Condon had their way inside, helping the Gators outrebound Arkansas 51-31 and win the battle in the paint. Billy Richmond III led the Razorbacks (21-8, 11-5) with 22 points, including 14 in the first 15 minutes. Darius Acuff Jr., who leads the SEC in scoring and assists, finished with 17 points and six assists. Florida guard Boogie Fland, facing his former team, was one of seven Gators in double figures and helped end Calipari's six-game winning streak in Gainesville. Florida has won six in a row against ranked teams and scored at least 90 points in each of those. It's the second-longest such streak in college basketball history. The longest mark is shared by UNLV and Loyola-Marymount, both in the 1989-90 season. Up next Arkansas: Hosts Texas on Wednesday night. NASHVILLE, Tenn. -- — Darius Acuff Jr. scored 37 points and No. 17 Arkansas held off Oklahoma 82-79 on Friday night to reach the Southeastern Conference Tournament semifinals. The third-seeded Razorbacks (24-8) won their third straight overall and will play No. 15 seed Mississippi in the semifinals after the Rebels upset No. 15 Alabama 80-79 earlier Friday night. Trevon Brazile added 12 points for Arkansas. Meleek Thomas, who came in averaging 15.4 points, was 2-of-10 shooting and finished with eight points. The Razorbacks were the last team to play at this tournament, while this was Oklahoma’s third game in as many nights. The game featured 16 lead changes and 11 ties, and Acuff, the SEC's scoring leader and league's player and freshman of the year, had 21 in the first half to keep Arkansas within 39-37 at halftime. Neither team led by more than four much of the second half, and Dayton Forsythe's layup tied it for the last time at 67 with 5:38 left. Malique Ewin's dunk put Arkansas ahead to stay with 5:17 left. The Razorbacks scored nine of the next 11, and Acuff's 3 with 2:27 remaining gave them their biggest lead at 76-69. The Sooners had a final chance after Acuff missed a second free throw with 10 seconds left. Nijel Pack's 3 missed and Arkansas got the ball with 0.6 seconds remaining. No. 11 seed Oklahoma (19-15) needed an upset in this game to strengthen its NCAA Tournament resume after losing nine straight early in league play. The Sooners' six-game winning streak was snapped. Pack led the Sooners with 19 points, Derrion Reid and Tae Davis each had 17 and Mohamed Wague had 13. 2026-03-15 - Arkansas: 86 vs Vanderbilt: 75
profile_summary:
Arkansas enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 4-seed with an impressive 26-8 record, having navigated one of college basketball's toughest schedules in the SEC. Against ranked opponents, the Razorbacks went an impressive 8-7, with quality wins over then-No. 6 Louisville (89-80 at home), then-No. 16 Texas Tech (93-86 on a neutral court), then-No. 19 Tennessee (86-75 at home), then-No. 21 Vanderbilt (93-68 at home), and, most recently, No. 22 Vanderbilt (86-75) in the SEC Tournament championship — Calipari's first conference title with the program. Their losses against ranked competition came against legitimate blue-blood programs — No. 4 Duke, No. 8 Houston, No. 5 Florida (a humbling 34-point blowout in Gainesville), and No. 25 Alabama in a heartbreaking 117-115 double-overtime road thriller — losses that reveal a ceiling when facing truly elite opponents but also demonstrate a team capable of trading punches at the highest level. Entering the tournament, Arkansas is scorching: their final five games produced wins over Texas (105-85), Missouri in overtime (88-84 without Acuff), Oklahoma (82-79), Ole Miss (93-90 in OT), and Vanderbilt (86-75), with the Razorbacks finishing as SEC Tournament champions and winners of six straight. The Missouri overtime win is particularly notable in that Arkansas gutted it out without star freshman Darius Acuff Jr., who missed the game with an undisclosed injury — the fact that Meleek Thomas (28 points), Malique Ewin (17 points), and Trevon Brazile (19 points and 9 rebounds) stepped up to seal the win speaks to legitimate depth and resilience.

Stylistically, Arkansas is a high-octane, fast-break-heavy offense that can erupt for 90-plus points on any given night — their 108-point demolition of South Carolina, 115-61 dismantling of Jackson State, and 105-85 blowout of Texas all underscore a team capable of taking over games in an instant. However, they can be defensively vulnerable, frequently allowing opponents to crawl back from large deficits, as seen in their near-collapse against Winthrop (84-83), a 117-115 double-OT loss to Alabama after leading by 14, and a late scare from Louisville after holding a 20-point halftime lead. The engine of this team is freshman Darius Acuff Jr., who is nothing short of spectacular — his 49-point performance in Tuscaloosa, six-game streak of 20-plus points, SEC Player and Freshman of the Year honors, and 37-point tournament quarterfinal effort against Oklahoma mark him as one of the most dynamic individual talents in the country. Acuff's undisclosed injury from the Missouri game bears monitoring as tournament play begins; if he is anything less than 100%, Arkansas's offensive ceiling drops considerably. Senior forward Trevon Brazile provides critical veteran balance — a career-high 28-point performance against Texas in his final home game and consistent double-double production confirm him as a reliable second option — while Thomas, Wagner, Richmond, and Ewin round out a rotation that has proven dangerous when everyone contributes. Location-wise, Arkansas is 8-0 at home in SEC play, with both home losses coming against Duke and Houston early in the year, and they have performed credibly on neutral courts — winning the SEC Tournament and covering in Dallas against Texas Tech. Their road record is spottier, with losses at Auburn, Georgia, and Florida suggesting they can be overwhelmed when a hostile environment compounds defensive lapses. This Arkansas team's realistic tournament ceiling is the Elite Eight: they have the star power, offensive firepower, championship-level momentum, and enough depth to beat anyone on a given night, but the pattern of surrendering large leads, inconsistency on defense, and vulnerability to truly elite programs — Florida and Duke exposed them badly — makes a Final Four run a stretch without Acuff being fully healthy and the defense tightening up when it matters most.

2026-03-15 - UC Irvine: 64 vs Hawai'i: 71
profile_summary:
Hawai'i enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 13-seed with a 24-8 record, having earned their bid by winning the Big West Conference Tournament championship — capping the season with a 71-64 upset of No. 1 seed UC Irvine in the title game after defeating Cal State Fullerton in the semifinals. Their résumé against quality competition is limited but not without merit: they nearly knocked off Oregon in a 60-59 season-opening road loss that came down to the final seconds, fell to Arizona State 83-76 at home after blowing a late second-half lead, and split with UC San Diego and UC Irvine during Big West play, suggesting a team capable of competing with mid-major elites but vulnerable to lapses down the stretch. Their five losses outside the tournament came in ways that reveal a recurring pattern — leads evaporating late, overtime collapses, and unexpected home defeats to teams like Cal Poly and Cal State Northridge — pointing to a team that is talented but inconsistency-prone. Over their final five regular-season and conference tournament games, Hawai'i went 4-1, with wins over UC Riverside (93-74), a narrow escape over Cal State Fullerton (87-85), a semifinal blowout of Cal State Fullerton (78-63), and the championship win over UC Irvine (71-64), with the only blemish being a 84-75 home loss to Long Beach State — a sequence that reflects a team heating up at the right time while still showing some vulnerability.

Stylistically, Hawai'i is a balanced, high-scoring mid-major that can put up 80-plus points on any given night and wins through offensive depth rather than a single transcendent star. Isaac Johnson is the engine — a versatile big who can score from the perimeter, crash the glass, and protect the rim — while Dre (Quandre) Bullock provides secondary scoring punch and Harry Rouhliadeff offers a reliable double-double threat on the wings. The Rainbow Warriors are at their best when spread the floor and getting to the free-throw line, as seen in their 100-point showcase against East Texas A&M and their efficient second-half runs in the conference tournament. However, they have been prone to stunning collapses — most notably allowing Arizona State to outscore them 26-11 over the final seven minutes to erase an eight-point lead — and have been beaten decisively on the road in Santa Barbara, San Diego, and Northridge, going a concerning 4-5 away from Honolulu in games where detailed summaries exist, while posting a dominant home record. Their Big West Tournament run on a neutral site in Las Vegas, including two straight wins by double digits and seven, is an encouraging counter-data point. This is a dangerous mid-major team with enough offensive firepower and recent momentum to pull a first-round upset, but their history of late-game collapses, limited résumé against high-major competition, and road/neutral-site inconsistency makes a run beyond the Round of 32 a long shot. **Realistic tournament ceiling: Round of 32 — a live upset pick in the first round, but likely a second-round exit against a top-four seed.**

2026-03-15 - Arkansas: 86 vs Vanderbilt: 75
profile_summary: Vanderbilt enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 5-seed with a 26-8 record — a résumé that reflects one of the more remarkable stories in college basketball this season. The Commodores opened the year on a historic 16-0 run, their best start since 2007-08, fueled by a nation-leading scoring offense that routinely cracked 100 points and featured six players averaging double figures, led by sophomore guard Tyler Tanner and dynamic guard Duke Miles. Against ranked opponents, Vanderbilt went an impressive 7-6: statement wins include a home victory over then-13th-ranked Alabama, road wins at Auburn, and — most impressively — back-to-back SEC Tournament wins over 4th-ranked Florida (91-74) and 25th-ranked Tennessee (75-68), demonstrating genuine big-game capability. Their losses to ranked opponents — Texas (80-64), Florida (98-94 at home), Arkansas (93-68), and the SEC Tournament final loss to Arkansas (86-75) — reveal a team that can get overwhelmed by elite length and physicality, particularly when their shooting goes cold in the second half. Recent form is a mixed but mostly encouraging picture: over their final five games, Vanderbilt went 3-2, with the two losses being a one-point heartbreaker at Missouri and the SEC Tournament final, sandwiched around three straight wins including the stunning upset of Florida and a regular-season win at Tennessee. Stylistically, this is a high-octane, 3-point-dependent offense that thrives in shootouts — Tanner, who closed the year with 34-point and 25-point performances and capped the SEC Tournament run with 20 against Florida, is playing at an elite level, and Miles has returned from a knee injury with back-to-back double-digit outings including a season-high 30 points against Tennessee. The concern is sustainability: Vanderbilt is capable of going cold from deep for extended stretches, as evidenced by their 26.7% second-half shooting in the Texas loss and a near-collapse against Oklahoma, and Devin McGlockton's flagrant foul issues at Missouri added unnecessary volatility. On location, Vanderbilt has been marginally stronger at home (Memorial Gym effect is real), but their neutral-site résumé is genuinely impressive — they won the Battle 4 Atlantis over Saint Mary's and VCU, and their two SEC Tournament upset wins came on a neutral floor in Nashville. Entering the tournament, this team carries a confident, high-energy identity: they are not a grind-it-out squad but rather a team that imposes pace and volume, with enough clutch playmaking from Tanner and Miles to stay in close games. Their ceiling is a legitimate Elite Eight run — explosive enough offensively to knock off higher seeds in early rounds, with proven big-game wins over elite competition, but ultimately too inconsistent defensively and too susceptible to physical, length-heavy opponents to be considered a Final Four lock.

profile_summary:
McNeese enters the 2026 NCAA tournament as a 12-seed with an impressive 28-5 record, having dominated the Southland Conference with a 19-3 conference mark and a league tournament championship. The Cowboys' résumé, however, comes with an important asterisk: their only true test against elite competition was a 112-71 blowout loss at No. 2 Michigan in late December, a game in which they trailed 60-29 at halftime and were never competitive. Beyond that outlier, McNeese's schedule was largely mid-major and low-major fare, making it difficult to project their ceiling against Power Conference–caliber opponents. Their five losses came against Santa Clara, Incarnate Word (twice), UT Rio Grande Valley, and Michigan — a mixed bag that raises some questions about consistency, particularly on the road or in neutral settings against mid-major peers. That said, the Cowboys clearly have a legitimate star in Larry Johnson, who has been a force all season — scoring 27 points with 12 rebounds and seven steals in the Southland Tournament semifinal against UT Rio Grande Valley, including the game-winning layup in triple overtime, before following it up with a dominant performance in the championship win over Stephen F. Austin. Johnson's ability to take over in clutch moments is the engine of this program.

Entering the tournament, McNeese is playing as well as any team in the country at the mid-major level, riding a nine-game winning streak that includes that exhausting triple-overtime thriller against UT Rio Grande Valley (84-80) and a wire-to-wire 76-59 dismantling of Stephen F. Austin in the Southland championship — a team that finished a game ahead of them in the regular season standings. The final five games in the data show wins by margins of 7, 17 (triple-OT excluded from margin context), 4, 11 (triple-OT), and 17 points, demonstrating a team that can win ugly and win convincingly. Tyshawn Archie provides a dangerous second scoring option off or alongside Johnson, shooting efficiently from three and capable of big nights — he posted 18 points, five assists, and six steals in the regular-season finale at Nicholls. Jerrell Colbert adds toughness inside with his rebounding and shot-blocking. The Cowboys' style is varied: they are capable of high-scoring blowouts (132-50, 102-66, 97-54), grind-out close wins (64-63 at Lamar, 66-64 over SFA), and clutch late-game heroics, suggesting a team with genuine competitiveness and resilience rather than a one-dimensional squad. What they have not demonstrated is the ability to stay in a game against a truly elite opponent, which is exactly what a potential 5-seed matchup in round one would present. McNeese is a genuinely dangerous 12-seed capable of pulling off a first-round upset on the strength of Johnson's star power and their current momentum, but the ceiling is realistically the Round of 32 — the Michigan blowout is too large a data point to ignore, and there is no evidence they can compete for 40 minutes against a high-major team executing at a tournament level.

summaries:
2025-11-04 - BYU: 71 vs Villanova: 66

LAS VEGAS -- — Freshman AJ Dybantsa had 21 points and six rebounds and eighth-ranked BYU defeated Villanova 71-66 Monday night in the Hall of Fame Series and season-opener for both teams. Dybantsa, one of the nation’s top freshman, shot 9 for 18 from the floor in his collegiate debut. Richie Saunders added 15 points and seven rebounds and Robert Wright III scored 14 points for the Cougars, who spoiled the regular-season debut of new Villanova coach Kevin Willard. Saunders extended his streak of made free throws to 29. That run dates to last season and is tied for second-longest in program history. The victory extended BYU’s regular-season win streak to nine games, its longest since 2019-20. The Wildcats, who endured a rash of injuries leading up to the start of the season, were led by Bryce Lindsay's 22 points. Christian Jeffrey added 11 points and Duke Brennan finished with eight points and 15 rebounds. BYU did a good job of taking care of the ball, finishing with just four turnovers. Meanwhile, the Wildcats committed 11 turnovers, which led to 17 points for the Cougars. Both teams struggled in the first half. The Cougars shot 36% (14 of 34) from the field, while Villanova was worse, hitting 12 of 35 (26%). BYU used a 23-13 run during an 11-minute span to open a 12-point lead with 4:25 left in the first half. The Cougars went into the locker room with a 42-32 halftime lead. Up next Villanova: Hosts Queens University of Charlotte on Saturday. BYU: Hosts Holy Cross on Saturday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. 2025-11-09 - BYU: 98 vs Holy Cross: 53

PROVO, Utah -- — Richie Saunders scored 20 points, freshman and top recruit AJ Dybantsa had 17 points and eight rebounds, and No. 8 BYU never trailed in a 98-53 win over Holy Cross on Saturday night. Five BYU players scored in double figures, including Robert Wright III with 15 points as the Cougars (2-0) shot 62% from the field and committed only six turnovers. Tyler Boston led Holy Cross (0-2) with 12 points and Aidan Richard added 11. BYU overwhelmed the Crusaders on both ends of the court. The Cougars outscored Holy Cross 62-28 in the paint and had a 21-8 edge in fast-break points. They also scored 24 points off 15 Crusaders turnovers. BYU opened the game with 10 straight points and never looked back. The Cougars carved out a 47-18 lead with 4:17 left before halftime following a 14-0 run. Dominique Diomande and Saunders combined for three straight dunks during the run. The Cougars missed their first seven 3-point field goal attempts before Dawson Baker knocked down a shot from the perimeter with 2:43 remaining in the first half. They went 1 of 10 overall from 3-point range before halftime. Holy Cross trailed by 46 points in the second half while shooting 13.6% (3 of 22) from 3-point range. The Crusaders started 0 for 15 from the perimeter before finally making an outside basket five minutes into the second half. Up Next Holy Cross is at Utah on Monday night. 2025-11-16 - UConn: 86 vs BYU: 84

BOSTON -- — Silas Demary Jr. scored 21 points and made a crucial steal to help third-ranked UConn hold off a late rally by No. 7 BYU for an 86-84 victory Saturday night. Tarris Reed Jr. added 21 points and eight rebounds. Alex Karaban also had 21 points for the Huskies (4-0), who began a five-game stretch that features four ranked opponents. Demary finished with seven assists, five rebounds and two steals. The game was played at TD Garden, home of the Boston Celtics, as part of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame Series. Highly touted BYU freshman forward AJ Dybantsa, who grew up just outside Boston and is considered a potential No. 1 pick in the NBA draft next summer, led the Cougars (3-1) with 25 points. Richie Saunders added 17 and Robert Wright III finished with 16. UConn led 82-77 when Dybantsa dropped in a layup with under a minute to play. Two free throws by Demary made it 84-79, but a 3-pointer by Dawson Baker cut the margin to two. Malachi Smith was fouled and hit one of two throws. The Cougars had a chance to tie the game, but Demary knocked the ball away from Wright. Solo Ball then made a free throw to push the lead back to four with 18 seconds left. The Huskies had 21 assists on 30 field goals. BYU managed just seven assists on 29 baskets. The Cougars played without starting point guard Kennard Davis Jr., arrested on suspicion of DUI following a crash in Utah on Thursday. After the game, coach Kevin Young said Davis has been suspended indefinitely for violating team rules. Davis was with the team Saturday but sat on the bench in street clothes. He started the first two games of the season before sitting out a win over Delaware on Tuesday with an injury. Cougars senior forward Keba Keita was helped off the court with 8:51 left in the first half after appearing to take a blow to his head while fighting for a loose ball. Up next UConn: Hosts No. 5 Arizona on Wednesday. BYU: Hosts No. 24 Wisconsin on Nov. 24. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. SALT LAKE CITY -- — Richie Saunders made five 3-pointers and scored 26 points to lead No. 9 BYU to a 98-70 victory over No. 23 Wisconsin on Friday. AJ Dybantsa added 18 points and six rebounds to help the Cougars (4-1) earn their first victory over a ranked opponent this season. Rob Wright III added 10 points and his 11 assists were three more than Wisconsin had as a team. Nick Boyd led Wisconsin with 16 points. Nolan Winter had 14 points and 14 rebounds, and John Blackwell and Braeden Carrington scored 14 apiece for the Badgers. Wisconsin struggled with prolonged shooting droughts, shooting just 32% from the field before halftime. The Badgers missed eight straight baskets over a 5 1/2-minute stretch and then had five consecutive misses over a three-minute span. BYU took advantage. The Cougars used a 13-0 run fueled by 3-pointers from Saunders, Wright and Tyler Mrus to take an 18-6 lead. Carrington made a pair of 3-pointers to trim the deficit to single digits. BYU responded with a 16-4 run. Back-to-back baskets from Dybantsa and a steal and dunk by Khadim Mboup put the Cougars up 34-18. Wisconsin cut the deficit to single digits after Dybantsa picked up his fourth foul just 2:10 into the second half, pulling with 56-49 on Winter’s putback. Saunders ended the rally with a layup and then made four 3-pointers to fuel a 31-9 run, extending the Cougars' lead to 87-58 with 4:32 left. The Badgers scored only nine points over a 10-minute stretch. Up next Wisconsin faces Providence in the Rady Children’s Invitational in San Diego on Thanksgiving Day. The Cougars trailed by four at halftime but Dybantsa opened the second half with a jumper and had 12 points by the time 10-0 and 7-0 runs gave BYU a 59-45 lead with 6:52 to play. Robert Wright III scored 17 points and Dybantsa added 16 with eight rebounds for the Cougars (5-1), who went 6 of 10 from 3-point range in the second half, when they shot 54.5% and made 13 of 18 free throws. The Hurricanes led 33-29 at halftime despite going 3 of 13 from 3-point range and 2 of 9 at the foul line. The Cougars shot 39% and missed their only two foul shots. There were five ties and eight lead changes before a 7-0 burst in the final minute gave Miami the lead. Henderson's three-point play made it 29-27. Ernest Udeh Jr. had a tipin and Donaldson had a put back after Udeh couldn't convert a three-point play. Wright slipped in a layup at the buzzer for BYU. Saunders made 9 of 15 shots from the floor, including 6 of 10 from 3-point range, and all five of his free throws for the Cougars (6-1). He also had eight rebounds and three steals. Wright hit 9 of 16 shots with two 3-pointers and added six assists and two steals. AJ Dybantsa scored 18 on 7-for-12 shooting. Amael L'Etang added 13 points and De'Shayne Montgomery 11. Bennett made five from beyond the arc in the first half, scoring 17 to help Dayton take a 36-30 lead into the break. Montgomery buried a 3-pointer to begin the second half for Dayton, but Saunders and Dybantsa had back-to-back layups and Wright hit from deep to spark a 15-0 run and the Cougars moved in front 45-39 with 16:24 remaining. BYU played with a lead until Bennett hit a 3-pointer in the middle of a 7-0 run to pull the Flyers even at 67-all with four minutes left. Wright and Heard traded 3-pointers before Saunders followed with a four-point play and the Cougars held on over the final 2:56. BYU shot 51.8% overall and 45.5% (10 for 22) from beyond the arc. Cal Baptist drew within five on a 3-pointer from Jordan Mueller, but the Lancers were unable to trim the deficit any further before BYU heated up again. Dybantsa and Keita combined for five baskets during a 19-5 run that extended the lead to 40-21 with 2:12 left before halftime. profile_summary:
BYU enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 6-seed with a 23-11 record — a résumé built on star power, dramatic moments, and a brutal late-season collapse that raises real durability questions. The Cougars went an impressive 7-8 against ranked opponents, with notable wins over #23 Wisconsin (98-70), #6 Iowa State (79-69), #10 Texas Tech (82-76 in the regular-season finale), and a tournament blowout of West Virginia — but suffered losses to #1 Arizona (twice), #14 Kansas, #8 Houston (twice), and unranked Oklahoma State and UCF, the latter two representing damaging defeats that exposed real defensive vulnerabilities. Their final five games tell a conflicted story: a 105-91 blowout of Kansas State fueled by a Dybantsa 40-point explosion, a revenge win over West Virginia, then a competitive 73-66 loss to Houston in the Big 12 quarterfinals playing on three days of fatigue — sandwiched around a three-game skid (losses to Cincinnati by 22, West Virginia by 8, and UCF by 13) that revealed a team capable of complete disengagement, particularly on the road and away from marquee moments. BYU is fundamentally an offense-first team that wins through star creation, three-point volume, and paint dominance, but folds badly when opponents impose pace, force turnovers, or hit them early — as West Virginia's 20-5 first-half run and UCF's 64-28 halftime advantage both demonstrated.

Freshman AJ Dybantsa is the unquestioned centerpiece and a legitimate NBA lottery pick, capable of taking over any game — his 43-point performance against Utah, 40-point Big 12 tournament explosion, and 36-point effort against Oklahoma State confirm he is tournament-ready at an elite level — but he has also gone quiet in critical moments, shooting 6-of-24 against Arizona and 1-of-4 in the first half at West Virginia. Robert Wright III is a dangerous second option who delivered back-to-back 30-point games against Baylor and Colorado and can go nuclear on any given night, while Kennard Davis Jr. provides a reliable perimeter threat off the bench. The devastating loss of Richie Saunders to a torn ACL in mid-February, however, gutted BYU's spacing and floor leadership at the worst possible time — the Cougars went 3-6 after his injury — and without him, the offense has leaned too heavily on Dybantsa while losing its rhythm against disciplined defenses. BYU plays with genuine resilience in neutral-site and high-stakes environments — their 22-point second-half comeback against Clemson in the Jimmy V Classic and their overtime win over Colorado both demonstrate championship-level toughness — but they are dangerously inconsistent, having now dropped games to unranked Cincinnati, UCF, and Oklahoma State down the stretch. The realistic ceiling for this BYU team is the Sweet 16: Dybantsa and Wright give them enough star power to knock off a 3-seed on any given night, but the loss of Saunders, recurring first-half defensive breakdowns, and a troubling pattern of flat performances against teams that exploit their turnovers make a run to the Elite Eight a significant ask.

2026-03-11 - Louisville: 62 vs SMU: 58

CHARLOTTE, N.C. -- — Ryan Conwell hit two key shots in the final 1:17 and finished with 16 points as No. 24 Louisville battled back to beat SMU 62-58 on Wednesday and advanced to the Atlantic Coast Conference tournament quarterfinals. Adrian Wooley added 14 points and Isaac McKneely had 10 for the Cardinals (23-9), who’ll face Miami on Thursday. Jarron Pierre Jr. had 17 points and Jaden Toombs added 13 points and nine rebounds for SMU (20-13), which led by six in the second half before Louisville stormed back behind three inside baskets from Vangelis Zougris. After a defensive struggle in the first half ended in a 26-all tie, SMU built its biggest lead at 41-35 on back-to-back 3s by Sam Walters and Pierre from the same spot in right corner. The Mustangs, playing their second game in two days, appeared content to patiently work time off the shot clock, extending every possession and shortening the game. But Louisville worked its way back after SMU's 7-foot-1 rim protector Samet Yigitoglu picked up his fourth foul with 7:51 left and was forced to the bench. That freed up Zougris to work in the middle, where he scored three key baskets in a two-minute stretch. Yigitoglu returned with the game tied at 56 and three minutes left and Pierre briefly put the Mustangs ahead with a driving layup. But after SMU's Corey Washington went down with a shoulder injury, Conwell took over. He hit a 3 to give the Cardinals the lead for good at 60-58 with 1:17 left. SMU's Boopie Miller missed a 3 at the other end and Conwell drove the left side of lane and scored on a left-handed layup with 29.4 seconds left to make it a two-possession game. Louisville played without star freshman Mikel Brown Jr., who is out for the tournament with a back injury. Up next SMU: Awaits to see if it will make the NCAA Tournament. Reneau was 5 of 9 from the field and 7 of 8 from the foul line after halftime. Tre Donaldson scored 14 points for Miami (25-7), which moves on to face No. 10 Virginia in Saturday’s semifinals. Ryan Conwell had 22 points and nine rebounds and J’Vonne Hadley added 19 points for the Cardinals (24-10). Miami avenged a 92-89 loss to Louisville just five days earlier. This time, points weren't as easy to come by. Louisville, playing its second game in two days after failing to secure a top-four seed and a double bye despite its national ranking, led 38-37 at the break after a physical first half that saw three Cardinals players leave with bloodied faces. The Hurricanes used a swarming defense to take control early in the second half. Dante Allen came up with a steal and fastbreak layup for a three-point play to give Miami a 49-41 lead. Miami scored 17 points off turnovers. The Cardinals clawed back, cutting the lead to one with 1:23 left when Hadley came up with a steal and Issac McKneely put back Hadley's miss off a fastbreak layup. But Reneau made a layup and Washington came up with the play of the game when he stole the ensuing inbounds pass and was fouled. He made both free throws to push the lead to five with less than a minute to play. Reneau added two free throws and Shelton Henderson sealed the win with a steal and powerful two-handed dunk. profile_summary: Louisville enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 6-seed with a 23-10 record — a résumé built on explosive offense, a star freshman, and enough quality wins to validate their seeding, though a rocky finish and a critical injury cloud the outlook. Against ranked opponents this season, the Cardinals went 6-9: notable wins include a home upset of No. 9 Kentucky behind Mikel Brown Jr.'s 29-point freshman debut, a road victory at No. 22 Miami to close the regular season, and a neutral-site ACC Tournament win over SMU, while losses to No. 4 Duke (twice, including an embarrassing 83-52 blowout in Durham), No. 20 Tennessee, No. 16 Virginia, No. 18 North Carolina, and No. 22 Miami in the ACC quarterfinals reveal real vulnerabilities against top-tier competition. Louisville's last five games were a mixed bag: wins over Georgia Tech (87-70), at Miami (92-89 in a wild comeback), and over SMU in the ACC Tournament (62-58), sandwiched around a three-point loss at Clemson and a five-point quarterfinal exit to Miami — hardly the trajectory of a team gathering steam. The Cardinals play a high-tempo, three-point-heavy style that can be dazzling when firing — Brown posted an ACC freshman-record 45 points against NC State, and the team has scored 100-plus points multiple times — but they are dangerously streaky from deep and prone to second-half collapses, as seen against North Carolina (surrendered a 16-point lead) and Wake Forest (blew a 15-point cushion before surviving at the free-throw line). The elephant in the room is Brown, Louisville's engine and top NBA draft prospect, who is out for the tournament with a recurring back injury that already cost him nine regular-season games; without him, the Cardinals went 5-4 and were regularly outclassed by elite defenses, leaning heavily on Ryan Conwell (who is capable of 20-plus but also went 4-of-16 and 6-of-21 in losses to Arkansas and Stanford) and the interior contributions of Sananda Fru. Louisville is demonstrably stronger at home — they are dominant in KFC Yum! Center and handled most non-conference opponents comfortably — but their road and neutral-site record is inconsistent, with the team routinely starting cold and spotting opponents early leads before scrambling back; their character shows in clutch moments (Wooley's game-winning 3 at Miami, Conwell's late heroics against SMU in Charlotte), but they cannot sustain that resilience against elite defenders. The verdict: without Mikel Brown Jr., Louisville is a dangerous but limited team — Conwell, McKneely, Fru, and Hadley can carry them past a lower seed in the first round, and their offense has enough firepower to pull off a second-round upset, but their ceiling is realistically the Round of 32 to Sweet 16. A deeper run would require their supporting cast to shoot consistently from three, eliminate second-half defensive lapses, and hope the bracket shields them from a Duke-or-Virginia-caliber opponent early — none of which are safe assumptions. This is a team that can make noise for a game or two, but is not built — at this moment, without its best player — for a Final Four run.

profile_summary:
South Florida enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as an 11-seed with a 25-8 record, a mark that reflects a genuinely impressive second half of the season after a rocky start. The Bulls opened the year struggling against quality competition — falling to Oklahoma State 103-95, VCU 78-66, and a ranked Alabama squad 104-93 — finishing November and December at just 6-5, exposing real defensive vulnerabilities against high-major opponents. However, the AAC regular season told a different story: South Florida went 14-3 in conference play, winning eight straight at one point and capturing the American Athletic Conference's top position in the loss column. The lone ranked opponent they faced was Alabama (No. 16 at the time), and the Bulls lost convincingly 104-93, as the Crimson Tide pulled away with a 17-4 second-half run — a concerning blueprint for how elite teams can handle them. Their conference tournament run produced dominant wins over Charlotte (86-64), Charlotte again (no surprise), and Wichita State (70-55), suggesting they arrive in the field with genuine momentum. The final five games across the summaries — wins over Memphis (96-89), Charlotte (83-60), Charlotte again (86-64), and Wichita State (70-55), capped by the road win at Memphis — show a team winning comfortably and with purpose, outscoring opponents by an average of roughly 25 points per game in that closing stretch.

South Florida is a high-volume offensive team built around multiple capable scorers, and the engine of this roster is **Wes Enis**, who has been one of the conference's most prolific offensive players down the stretch — posting 29 points at Memphis, 25 points twice against Tulane, 32 points against Tulsa (with 10 threes), and 16 points with a double-double against Wichita State. **Izaiyah Nelson** is the team's most complete two-way player, a physical presence who contributed 25 points against UTSA, a 24-point, 13-rebound double-double against Tulane, seven steals against Wichita State, and five blocks in the UAB overtime loss — he is a matchup problem at multiple levels. **Joseph Pinion** is a dangerous three-point specialist who erupted for 20 points on six-of-eleven from deep against Oklahoma State and has shown the ability to carry the offense on certain nights, while **Josh Omojafo** provides a versatile inside-out threat and has posted multiple double-doubles. The Bulls' offensive identity is a fast-paced, high-scoring system capable of erupting for 100-plus points — they scored 95 or more in each of their first five games — but this same style creates defensive exposure, and elite teams have exploited it all season. In close games, South Florida has shown a troubling pattern: they lost to UAB in overtime (109-106 after blowing a halftime lead), lost to Wichita State in overtime (86-85 on a go-ahead layup with 20 seconds left), and dropped a heartbreaker at Temple (79-78 on a tip-in with six seconds remaining) — three winnable games that slipped away in the final moments. Conversely, they play with evident confidence and swagger when rolling, often building large first-half leads and sustaining them. At home in Tampa, South Florida appears especially dangerous, but on the road they have struggled: early-season losses at Oklahoma State and VCU, and conference losses in overtime scenarios on neutral energy. Their résumé against ranked opponents is 0-1, with no signature wins to anchor their tournament case. **South Florida's realistic ceiling is the Round of 32**: dangerous enough as an 11-seed to knock off a 6-seed in the first round behind Enis's scoring and Nelson's two-way energy, but their late-game collapse tendencies, lack of proven performance against elite competition, and defensive vulnerabilities make a Sweet 16 run a stretch against the caliber of opponent they would face in the second weekend.
