profile_summary: Alabama enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 4-seed with a 23-9 record — a résumé built on explosive offense, elite perimeter shooting, and a genuinely brutal non-conference schedule that simultaneously proved the Tide's ceiling and exposed their floor. Against ranked opponents this season, Alabama went 8-9: statement wins over then-#5 St. John's at Madison Square Garden, #11 Illinois at the United Center, #11 Vanderbilt on the road, and #22 Tennessee in Knoxville stand out as legitimate elite-level victories, while losses to #2 Purdue, #12 Gonzaga, #1 Arizona, and #19 Florida — the latter two by a combined 44 points — reveal a team that can be overwhelmed physically when it cannot shoot its way back into a game. The Tide rank among the nation's leaders in 3-pointers made, averaging over 12 per game, and have strung together nine or more consecutive games with double-digit makes from deep; when that shot is falling, Alabama is nearly impossible to guard, as evidenced by their 117-115 double-overtime thriller over #20 Arkansas in which Labaron Philon Jr. scored a career-high 35 points and Aiden Sherrell added 26. Looking at the final five games, Alabama went 3-2: a 100-75 blowout of Mississippi State (with Philon sidelined), a gutsy 71-69 road win at #22 Tennessee on a Philon jumper with 22.8 seconds left, a deflating 98-88 loss at Georgia in which Kanon Catchings torched them for 32, a bounce-back 96-84 home win over Auburn in the regular-season finale, and then a stunning 80-79 first-round SEC Tournament upset loss to 15th-seeded Ole Miss — a team Alabama had beaten by 19 in the regular season — that raises serious questions about consistency and composure at the worst possible moment. Alabama's identity is high-wire offense: they win in shootouts, come back from deficits through hot shooting runs, and occasionally blow teams out with 22-3-pointer explosions, but they are also turnover-prone (18 turnovers in the Florida blowout, five from Philon alone in the Texas loss), and they are consistently destroyed on the glass — outrebounded by Purdue 52-28, Gonzaga 48-36, Arizona 52-32, and Florida decisively — which becomes a compounding problem when 3-point shooting goes cold. Philon, the SEC's leading scorer averaging 22 points per game, is the engine of everything Alabama does; his ability to create off the dribble, hit pull-up jumpers, and push the pace makes him a genuine first-team All-SEC talent, while Aden Holloway provides a complementary scoring punch from deep (career-high 26 points twice this season), and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. gives Alabama a dangerous tertiary option, going 7-of-10 from three against Missouri. The injury situation has been a persistent subplot all season — Holloway, Wrightsell, Keitenn Bristow, Jalil Bethea, and even Philon have missed games at various points — and the eligibility circus surrounding 7-footer Charles Bediako ultimately ended when his injunction was denied, leaving Alabama without a true interior presence to counter elite frontcourts. At home, Alabama is dominant, but on the road and at neutral sites the picture is messier: they beat St. John's and Illinois in neutral/road settings yet were blown out by Arizona, Florida, and lost to Georgia and Ole Miss in their final four away-from-home appearances. **Alabama is a dangerous, unpredictable team capable of beating anyone on a hot-shooting night, but their inability to rebound, tendency to collapse against elite frontcourts, chronic turnover issues, and the psychological weight of that late SEC Tournament implosion against Ole Miss cap their realistic ceiling at the Sweet 16** — with a first- or second-round exit equally plausible if they run into a physical, defense-first team that can take them off the 3-point line before Philon catches fire.

profile_summary:
Hofstra enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 13-seed with a 24-10 record, a mark that understates the program's legitimate quality — particularly their stunning mid-season stretch against Power Conference competition. The Pride's most compelling résumé items are back-to-back ACC wins in December: an 80-73 road demolition of Pittsburgh behind Cruz Davis's career-high 36 points, followed immediately by a 70-69 stunner at Syracuse on a German Plotnikov 3-pointer with 34 seconds left, sealed by Victory Onuetu's game-saving block. Those wins give Hofstra genuine big-game credibility that most 13-seeds simply cannot claim. Their early-season struggles — opening 0-2 with losses to UCF and Iona, both by single digits — reflect a team still finding its identity, but the mid-season 0-5 skid (Games 18-22, spanning losses to Stony Brook, Elon, North Carolina A&T, William & Mary, and Charleston) is a legitimate red flag, exposing vulnerabilities when Davis is contained and the supporting cast goes cold. Entering the tournament, however, Hofstra is playing its best basketball: over their final five games (CAA quarterfinal, semifinal, and championship wins over William & Mary 92-61, Towson 68-65 in overtime, and Monmouth 75-69, plus regular-season wins at Northeastern 82-68 and at home over Stony Brook 67-58), Hofstra went 5-0 and won the CAA Tournament outright, demonstrating genuine momentum.

Cruz Davis is unquestionably one of the most dangerous mid-major scorers in the country, capable of single-handedly dismantling opponents — his 36-point outburst at Pitt and 30-point CAA quarterfinal performance against William & Mary illustrate his elite ceiling — and Preston Edmead complements him as a clutch secondary creator, hitting the game-winning 3-pointer in overtime of the CAA semifinal and consistently delivering in big moments throughout the year. The supporting cast — Plotnikov as a streaky but capable floor-spacer, James Patterson as a reliable physical presence, and Joshua DeCady as a tertiary scorer — provides enough depth to win games when Davis is off, though Hofstra's mid-season collapse demonstrated that when neither Davis nor Edmead is efficient simultaneously, the offense can stall badly. Stylistically, this is a team that wins through offensive firepower and clutch shot-making rather than defensive dominance, thriving in high-scoring environments and proving capable of dramatic comebacks (the Towson overtime win, the late Syracuse victory), but also prone to close losses when opponents match their scoring punch. Their location splits paint an interesting picture: some of the team's best performances have come away from home (the Pitt blowout, the Northeastern road win, the neutral-site CAA run), suggesting they are not overly dependent on home-crowd energy — a meaningful trait entering a neutral-site tournament. The realistic ceiling for this Hofstra squad is the Round of 32, with a puncher's chance at the Sweet 16 if Davis catches fire: talented enough and battle-tested enough against Power Conference competition to pull a first-round upset, but too inconsistent across a full 40 minutes — and too susceptible to defensive breakdowns against disciplined high-major offenses — to be considered a genuine Elite Eight threat.

name: Kansas
summaries:
2025-11-04 - Kansas: 94 vs Green Bay: 51

LAWRENCE, Kan. -- — Freshman Darryn Peterson scored 21 points in 22 minutes in his debut to lead No. 19 Kansas past Green Bay 94-51 in the season opener for both teams Monday night. Peterson shot 7 of 11 from the field, including 3 for 7 from 3-point range, before sitting most of the second half. Kansas' Flory Bidunga led all scorers with 23 points on 9-of-11 shooting from the field and 5-of-6 sniping from the line. Melvin Council added 10 points for Kansas, which was 33 of 58 from the field (56.9%). Green Bay was led by Marcus Hall's 17 points. The Jayhawks, who led by 21 points at the break, put any thoughts of a Green Bay comeback to rest by scoring the first seven points of the second half. The Jayhawks extended their lead throughout the second half and their final 43-point margin was their largest of the game. Peterson made an impact early. His first bucket, a 3-pointer from the top of the key at the 18:31 mark, put Kansas in front and the Jayhawks never looked back. He also had two steals before the first media timeout. He finished the first half with 18 points, three rebounds, three assists and two steals. Bidunga picked up his second foul with 6:44 left, which earned him a spot on the bench. He finished the half with 16 points. He was 6 for 6 from the field and 4 for 4 from the line. The Jayhawks were 17 of 29 from the field in the first half, and missed 11 of their 14 3-point attempts. They still led 48-27 at intermission. Green Bay was led by C.J. O’Hara's nine points before halftime. The Phoenix hit just 34.6% of their shots. Up next Green Bay: At Buffalo on Friday. Kansas: At No. 25 North Carolina on Friday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-08 - North Carolina: 87 vs Kansas: 74

CHAPEL HILL, N.C. -- — Freshman Caleb Wilson had 24 points, big man Henri Veesaar added 20 and No. 25 North Carolina dominated the second half to pull away from No. 19 Kansas 87-74 in Friday night's battle of college basketball bluebloods. Senior Seth Trimble added 13 of his 17 after halftime, proving to be a catalyst for the Tar Heels (2-0) in taking control of the second-half tempo as UNC roared out of the break. North Carolina made 18 of its first 23 second-half shots — including back-to-back push-the-tempo scores by Trimble that had Kansas coach Bill Self first waving his hands in frustration and then burning a quick timeout barely two minutes in. Kyan Evans added 12 points after a scoreless first half for the Tar Heels, who went from shooting 33% before the break to making 24 of 36 shots (66.7%) after halftime to lead by as many as 16 on the way to scoring 58 second-half points. That 58-point output was tied for the second-most allowed in any half by Kansas under its 23rd-year coach, according to SportRadar. Kansas star freshman Darryn Peterson had 22 points to lead the Jayhawks (1-1), who led by 10 in the first half and 37-29 at the break. The Jayhawks entered Friday having won five straight meetings between programs with a combined 10 NCAA titles, including in the 2008 Final Four, the 2012 NCAA Elite Eight and the 2022 national championship game. The Jayhawks won last year's meeting at Allen Fieldhouse despite blowing a 20-point lead and were playing their first game in Chapel Hill. Up next Kansas: The Jayhawks return home to face Texas A&M-Corpus Christi on Tuesday. UNC: The Tar Heels continue a five-game homestand to open the season with Tuesday's visit from Radford. ------ This story has been corrected to show UNC's ranking is No. 25, not No. 24. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. 2025-11-15 - Kansas: 76 vs Princeton: 57

LAWRENCE, Kan. -- — Flory Bidunga scored a career-high 25 points, hitting 10 of his 11 field goal attempts, as No. 25 Kansas beat Princeton 76-57 on Saturday. Kansas (3-1) used a 17-3 run midway through the second half to blow open a close game. Tre White added 18 points for the Jayhawks. Princeton (2-2) was led by Dalen Davis with 21 points. Jack Stanton had 15 and Jackson Hicke added 12. After Kansas opened up a 10-point lead at the start of the second half, Stanton hit a pair of 3-pointers, one on a 4-point play, to trim the deficit to just 39-36. The Jayhawks had trouble pulling away, and when Davis hit a 3-pointer with 14:23 left, the lead was just 44-43. But when Jayden Dawson made a 3-pointer less than two minutes later, Kansas again led by double digits. The Jayhawks' largest lead was 73-51. Princeton played at its pace in the first half, holding an 18-13 lead at the 10:44 mark. A 9-0 run that took more than five minutes gave Kansas the lead, but the Tigers stayed close. Kansas finished the half on a 7-2 run and led 37-29 at halftime. Bidunga stepped up late in the half and led all scorers with 15 points. Davis led Princeton with 13 points. Up Next Both clubs will be in action Tuesday night. The Tigers take on Iona in New Rochelle, N.Y., while Kansas will face Duke in the State Farm Champions Classic in New York.

2025-11-19 - Duke: 78 vs Kansas: 66

NEW YORK -- — Cameron Boozer had 18 points and 10 rebounds, twin brother Cayden made a couple of big second-half baskets and fifth-ranked Duke outlasted No. 24 Kansas, defeating the undermanned Jayhawks 78-66 on Tuesday night in the annual Champions Classic. Isaiah Evans scored 16 points and Patrick Ngongba had 13 on 4-of-4 shooting for the Blue Devils (5-0). The Jayhawks (3-2) were still without their best player, freshman Darryn Peterson, the nation's top recruit and potentially the top pick in next year's NBA draft, because of a lingering hamstring injury. Peterson has missed three consecutive games since hurting himself during a shootaround. Peterson's absence was enough to make a difference at Madison Square Garden even though the Blue Devils were less consistent than in their previous four victories. Kansas rattled Duke with some fast-break points early before getting worn down by foul trouble and a depth disparity. All five Kansas starters had at least one foul and three had two apiece in the first half, and Duke closed it out on a 17-5 run to lead by eight. The Jayhawks trimmed their deficit to 67-64 on Melvin Council Jr.'s 3-pointer with 4:59 left. The foul trouble showed in some tired legs. Flory Bidunga picked up his fourth with more than seven minutes left, and Bryson Tiller fouled out with 3:04 remaining. Evans hit a 3 coming out of the under-4-minute timeout, and Cameron Boozer scored four of the eight points in an 8-0 run by Duke to pull away. Tre White tried at every turn to keep Kansas in the game, scoring 23 points and grabbing nine rebounds. Up next Kansas: Faces Notre Dame on Monday night in each team's opening game of the Players Era tournament in Las Vegas. Duke: Hosts Niagara on Friday night. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-24 - Kansas: 71 vs Notre Dame: 61

White, a transfer from Illinois, had 23 points and 11 rebounds to cap his lone regular season in Lawrence, while Council — who arrived from St. Bonaventure — nearly had a triple-double with 17 points, eight rebounds and 10 assists in his final game at the Phog. 2026-03-14 - Houston: 69 vs Kansas: 47

KANSAS CITY, Mo. -- — Kingston Flemings scored 21 points, fellow freshman Chris Cenac Jr. had 17 points and 14 rebounds, and fifth-ranked Houston rolled past poor-shooting and lackluster No. 14 Kansas 69-47 in the semifinals of the Big 12 Tournament on Friday night. The second-seeded Cougars (28-5) held the third-seeded Jayhawks without a field goal for more than 10 minutes at point in the game, ultimately breezing into another championship matchup with second-ranked Arizona on Saturday night. Houston beat the Wildcats 72-64 last year for its first Big 12 Tournament title. Darryn Peterson had 14 points to lead the Jayhawks (23-10), who had beaten the Cougars in their only regular-season matchup. But the Jayhawks were a whole lot better that night in Allen Fieldhouse than they were at T-Mobile Center, where they were 6 for 31 from the field in the second half and shot 24.6% overall in their lowest-scoring game of the season. With the relentless defense that's become a hallmark of coach Kelvin Sampson, the Cougars controlled the game right from the start, making things especially difficult for Kansas big men Flory Bidunga and Bryson Tiller in the paint. Most of the Jayhawks' offense came from Peterson, the potential No. 1 pick in the NBA draft. He had 12 at the break. Things really unraveled for Kansas in the second half, though. Houston forced Bill Self's team into missing its first 16 shots while going on a 22-2 run of its own spanning halftime. Even the bucket that mercifully ended the drought, a dunk by Bidunga with 10:42 to go, nearly rattled its way back out of the basket. Houston led 51-31 at that point before coasting into its second consecutive championship game. Up next Houston lost to the No. 1 seed Wildcats at home on Feb. 21 in their only meeting of the regular season. Kansas will likely be a No. 4 seed when the NCAA Tournament is revealed on Sunday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. profile_summary: Kansas enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a No. 4 seed with a 23-10 record — a résumé that reflects genuine Big 12 toughness but also a team that has been maddeningly inconsistent when it matters most. Against ranked opponents, the Jayhawks went an impressive 9-9, with signature wins over No. 2 Iowa State (84-63), No. 1 Arizona at Allen Fieldhouse (82-78), No. 5 Houston (69-56), No. 13 BYU (90-82), and No. 11 Texas Tech, demonstrating they are absolutely capable of beating anyone in the country on a given night. However, those highs are offset by damaging losses to unranked UCF, Cincinnati, Arizona State, and West Virginia — teams Kansas had no business losing to — and a brutal 47-point output in a 69-47 Big 12 semifinal shellacking at the hands of Houston, which stands as perhaps the most alarming result of their season. Location has been a defining factor: Kansas is a fortress at Allen Fieldhouse, where they toppled Arizona and Houston, but they have been a different, diminished team away from Lawrence, going winless in their final three true road games and shooting a dismal 24.6% overall in that Big 12 tournament loss. Their style of play is a mixed identity — capable of dominant wire-to-wire performances, explosive scoring runs, and clutch comebacks (see the 104-100 overtime win over TCU), but also prone to inexplicable second-half collapses and cold shooting stretches that leave them vulnerable against elite defensive teams. Freshman phenom Darryn Peterson is both the team's greatest asset and its greatest source of anxiety entering March: the projected No. 1 NBA draft pick is averaging 25 points per game in appearances but has missed 11 games this season due to a cascading series of hamstring, calf, ankle, and flu issues, and his availability and durability remain the central question for this team's ceiling. When Peterson is healthy and locked in — 32 points in the TCU comeback, 27 in the Senior Day blowout of Kansas State, 24 in the Arizona rematch — Kansas is a legitimate threat to reach the Final Four. Supporting him is a legitimately deep and versatile cast: Flory Bidunga is a two-way force averaging 18-plus points in big games with elite shot-blocking and interior dominance; Tre White provides veteran scoring and rebounding punch as a double-double threat; and Melvin Council Jr. has the capacity for transcendent outings, as evidenced by his 36-point, nine-three-pointer career night against NC State. The team carries a scrappy, resilient identity — they have proven they can fight back from double-digit deficits — but that energy has been inconsistent, and they showed alarming flatness and disengagement in the Big 12 tournament semifinal, their worst performance of the season on the biggest stage available to them before March. The recent five-game stretch tells a complicated story: a blowout 104-85 Senior Day win over Kansas State, followed by a hard-fought Big 12 quarterfinal win over TCU (78-73), and then the stunning 69-47 semi-final collapse against Houston — a team Kansas had beaten by 13 just weeks earlier at home — bookended by earlier road losses at Arizona (84-61) and Arizona State (70-60), painting a picture of a team that is slumping and fragile away from Allen Fieldhouse heading into the tournament. Kansas is not a national title contender in their current form, but they remain a legitimate and dangerous threat capable of making a deep run if Peterson stays healthy, and their proven ability to beat No. 1 seeds means no team should feel comfortable drawing them. Their realistic tournament ceiling is the Elite Eight: the talent and big-game experience are present for a deep run, but the injury uncertainty surrounding Peterson, the alarming road/neutral-site struggles, and the capacity for inexplicable offensive breakdowns make a Final Four feel like the ceiling of the ceiling rather than the expectation.

Johnson made 9 of 11 foul shots and reserve Alon Michaeli added 11 points for the Buffaloes who shot 45% (24 of 53) including 38% (6 of 16) from 3-point range. Dominique Daniels Jr. scored 25 points with the help of 10-of-11 shooting from the foul line, and Martel Williams scored 14 points for the Lancers. Jordan Muller's layup with 13:03 left gave the Lancers a 50-48 lead after a Michaeli 3 tied it at 48. Daniels made it a four-point lead with 12:28 left, but the Lancers failed to score until 4:58 remained at which point Colorado took control with a 16-0 stretch and led 64-54 after Bangot Dak made a 3 with 5:12 left. Williams converted a three-point play and Jonathan Griman had a dunk with 4:11 left to get Cal Baptist within 64-59. The Lancers pulled within 70-66 on Daniels' personal 5-0 run that included a 3 and a layup but never got closer. It's the first time in 76 years — the 1949-50 season — that Colorado has started a season with eight straight wins. Up Next California Baptist travels to face ninth-ranked BYU on Wednesday. SALT LAKE CITY -- — AJ Dybantsa and Keba Keita each scored 22 points to lead No. 9 BYU to a 91-60 victory over California Baptist on Wednesday night. Keita added 14 rebounds to help the Cougars (7-1) win their fourth straight neutral-site game — though this one was played in their home state about 45 miles from campus. Robert Wright III had 15 points and 11 assists. BYU shot 55% from the field and forced 14 turnovers. The Cougars totaled 15 points off turnovers and outscored Cal Baptist 48-28 in the paint. Slowing down Dybantsa and Keita quickly became a problem for the Lancers. The two combined for 13 field goals and 28 points by halftime. Their baskets sparked a pair of huge runs that put BYU in control early. Back-to-back buckets from Keita and Dybantsa ignited a 10-0 spurt that put the Cougars up 14-5. Cal Baptist drew within five on a 3-pointer from Jordan Mueller, but the Lancers were unable to trim the deficit any further before BYU heated up again. Dybantsa and Keita combined for five baskets during a 19-5 run that extended the lead to 40-21 with 2:12 left before halftime. The Cougars held Cal Baptist to two baskets over a nine-minute stretch while forcing six turnovers. BYU made 10 straight shots, culminating in consecutive baskets from Dybantsa, to expand its advantage to 70-39 with 11:57 remaining. Up next California Baptist plays at Utah on Saturday. profile_summary:
California Baptist enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 13-seed with a 25-8 record, a résumé that reflects a team that earned its way in the hard way — through a grueling non-conference gauntlet and a competitive WAC campaign. The Lancers were tested early and often against high-major competition, going 0-3 against Big 12 opponents (losses to Colorado 78-70, BYU 91-60, and Utah 91-85), with the BYU defeat standing out as a genuine blowout where they were outscored 48-28 in the paint and committed 14 turnovers. Those early struggles against elite competition raise legitimate concerns about how Cal Baptist would match up against a high-major NCAA Tournament opponent. That said, the Lancers closed the regular season with clear momentum, finishing 12-5 in WAC play and winning the conference tournament on back-to-back nights — a 86-72 semifinal win over Utah Tech followed by a nail-biting 63-61 championship victory over Utah Valley, earning their automatic bid. Their final five games produced results of: a 19-point rout of Abilene Christian (87-48), a road win over Southern Utah (82-75), a regular-season finale win over Utah Tech (86-72 in the WAC semis), and the championship squeaker over Utah Valley — a pattern that shows a team peaking at the right time but still winning close ones by the thinnest of margins.

The identity of this Cal Baptist team runs almost entirely through one player: Dominique Daniels Jr., who is simply carrying this program on his back entering March. He scored 41 points in the WAC semifinal, 47 points (including eight in overtime) in a January win over Utah Valley, 32 against Southern Utah, 31 against Utah, and 28 against UT Arlington — a sustained run of elite individual production that makes him one of the more dangerous mid-major scorers in the bracket. Martel Williams provides a capable secondary option, and Jayden Jackson's ability to get hot from three (five 3-pointers in the WAC semifinal) gives the offense a second dimension, but if Daniels is neutralized, this team's offensive ceiling drops sharply. Cal Baptist appears to thrive at home (strong WAC record in Riverside) and struggled badly on the road against quality opponents, going 0-3 in road Big 12 games by a combined margin of 53 points. The team carries a scrappy, resilient identity — capable of dramatic comebacks like Daniels' buzzer-tying layup to force overtime against Utah Valley — but also prone to flat performances when overwhelmed athletically, as seen in the 91-60 BYU loss. Their WAC Tournament run shows genuine confidence and big-moment composure from Daniels, but a 13-seed facing a probable 4-seed Power Conference opponent will test them in ways the WAC never did. **Verdict: Cal Baptist is a dangerous first-round upset threat — Daniels is good enough to steal a game against the right matchup — but the lack of frontcourt depth, overreliance on one star, and 0-3 record against major-conference competition make a second-round appearance their realistic ceiling. Expect them to be live in Game 1, but unlikely to advance past the Round of 32.**

LUBBOCK, Texas -- — Christian Anderson had career highs with 34 points and 11 assists, leading No. 10 Texas Tech to a 98-60 victory over Lindenwood on Tuesday night as the Red Raiders extended their nonconference home winning streak to 39 games. Texas Tech, which has the highest preseason ranking in school history coming off the program's third Elite Eight appearance, was without preseason Big 12 player of the year JT Toppin and LeJuan Watts, a Washington State transfer who is the preseason newcomer of the year. Toppin and Watts were listed with lower-body injuries, as were two more transfers in Josiah Moseley (Villanova) and Luke Bamgboye (Virginia Commonwealth). Donovan Atwell scored 22 points, with all of his shots coming from 3-point range. The UNC Greensboro transfer was 6 of 13 from deep and 4 of 5 on free throws. Mekhi Cooper scored 15 points for Lindenwood, and Milos Nenadic had 12 points and 11 rebounds before fouling out. The Lions, who shot 31%, were without their top two scorers in Anias Futrell and 2024-25 Ohio Valley Conference freshman of the year Jadis Jones. Nolan Groves had 16 rebounds with seven points in his Texas Tech debut, while fellow freshman Jaylen Petty had nine points, eight rebounds and five assists. Anderson, who had seven rebounds and just one turnover, hit a 3-pointer to put Texas Tech ahead for good at 16-14 and start a 21-5 run that ended with his layup on a fast break for a 34-19 lead. 2026-03-12 - Texas Tech: 53 vs Iowa State: 75

KANSAS CITY, Mo. -- — Joshua Jefferson had 18 points and 13 rebounds, longtime running mate Tamin Lipsey scored 20 and seventh-ranked Iowa State used a big second-half run to bury No. 16 Texas Tech 75-53 on Thursday in the quarterfinals of the Big 12 Tournament. The fifth-seeded Cyclones (27-6) fell behind by 12 early in the game, pulled ahead by halftime, then used a 19-3 run shortly out of the break to earn a Friday night date with eighth-seeded UCF or second-ranked and No. 1 seed Arizona for a spot in the title game. LeJuan Watts had led the Red Raiders (22-10) with 12 points. Christian Anderson had 10 before he appeared to strain a muscle while slipping on the glass court being used by the Big 12 for its postseason tournament with about 8 1/2 minutes left in the game. Several players in both the women's and men's tourneys have complained of its slick nature. The teams traded haymakers to start the game — No. 4 seed Texas Tech opened on a 17-5 run and Iowa State countered a few minutes later with a 15-2 charge of its own — before the game settled into a back-and-forth battle between top 20 teams. There were nine lead changes in the first half, and Jamarion Batemon's buzzer-beating 3 gave the Cyclones a 36-33 halftime lead. Iowa State turned up the pressure another level in the second half, holding the Red Raiders to just two field goals over the first 9 1/2 minutes. And on the other end of the floor, Texas Tech had no answer for Jefferson, the 6-foot-9, 240-pound All-Big 12 forward, whose ability to split defenders and power his way to the basket had Grant McCasland's team thoroughly frustrated. That's when Iowa State, which set a Big 12 Tournament record by beating Arizona State by 49 in the second round, put together its long run against the Red Raiders and assumed control of the game. Up next Iowa State beat UCF 87-57 on Jan. 20 at Hilton Coliseum but lost 73-57 at Arizona on March 2. Texas Tech hopes to stay on the 4-seed line for the NCAA tourney on Selection Sunday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here. profile_summary: Texas Tech enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 5-seed with a 22-10 record — a résumé that screams "dangerous but damaged," built on elite moments and haunted by a devastating late-season injury. Against ranked opponents, the Red Raiders went an impressive 7-8, with signature wins over then-No. 3 Duke (an 82-81 comeback from 17 down at Madison Square Garden), No. 1 Arizona on the road in overtime, No. 4 Iowa State on the road (82-73), and No. 11 BYU at home, while also absorbing losses to No. 1 Purdue (a 30-point blowout), No. 7 Houston (twice), and No. 17 Arkansas. The final five games, however, tell a turbulent story: a wire-to-wire blowout of Kansas State (100-72) and a road upset of Iowa State (82-73) were followed by two losses — a home upset to TCU (65-73) and a road defeat at BYU (76-82) — before getting thoroughly handled by Iowa State (53-75) in the Big 12 Tournament quarterfinals, where Christian Anderson also appeared to strain a muscle late in the game. That closing stretch — 2-3 in the final five, including a 22-point beatdown to end the tournament — leaves Texas Tech entering March on a decidedly cold note. The elephant in the room is JT Toppin, the preseason AP All-American and Big 12 Player of the Year who tore his ACL at Arizona State on February 18th after averaging 21.8 points and 10.8 rebounds with 16 double-doubles; his absence has fundamentally altered this team's ceiling and interior presence. Without Toppin, the offense devolves almost entirely onto Christian Anderson — a legitimate star who put up 31 points and 11 rebounds against Cincinnati post-injury — and three-point specialist Donovan Atwell, who broke the single-season school record with 115 made threes and ranks among the nation's elite from deep. Texas Tech's identity is built on perimeter volume — they tied their program record with 17 made threes at Baylor, and are at their best when Anderson is orchestrating and Atwell is hot — but they are dangerously one-dimensional without Toppin to anchor the interior, as illustrated by BYU outscoring them 48-14 in the paint in the regular-season finale. The Red Raiders are 14-2 at home, clutch and confident in Lubbock, but have been erratic on the road and in neutral settings; their neutral-site record is a mixed bag: a tournament championship run that ended in a 30-point loss to Purdue, a stunning win over Duke at Madison Square Garden, and a Big 12 Tournament exit in the quarterfinals. They show genuine resilience — mounting comeback wins over Duke and Iowa State — but are also collapse-prone when their shooting goes cold, and the BYU loss (a lead squandered when Toppin was already gone) and Big 12 Tournament exit suggest a team that can look elite for long stretches and then completely unravel. **Texas Tech's realistic tournament ceiling is the Round of 32 or, at best, a Sweet 16 appearance against a favorable draw.** Without Toppin, there simply is not enough interior resistance or consistent closing ability to trust this team against elite competition for four consecutive games, and a 2-3 finish to the season with Anderson's health now in question makes them a genuinely risky proposition even in the first round.

profile_summary:
Akron enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 12-seed with an impressive 29-5 record, having dominated the Mid-American Conference at 17-1 in the regular season before winning the MAC Tournament championship. Their résumé against quality opposition is modest but notable: a competitive road loss to then-No. 2 Purdue (79-97), where they were outrebounded 45-26 and exposed on the interior, and a neutral-site loss to Murray State (100-115), where they surrendered 115 points and got outscored by 10 in the second half. Those two losses to genuinely good programs reveal a defensive ceiling that could be punishing in the tournament. Outside of non-conference play, non-MAC losses came against Yale (94-97), Troy (69-79), and Miami (OH) — losses that underscore a tendency to occasionally go flat against opponents they should handle. However, Akron has responded impressively every time, never losing more than one game in a row all season. Their final five games — a 94-55 demolition of Northern Illinois, a gritty 73-70 MAC Tournament quarterfinal win over Buffalo, a 75-68 semifinal win over Kent State, and a 79-76 championship win over Toledo — demonstrate that they are peaking at exactly the right moment, closing out close games while also showing they can blow teams out when dialed in.

Akron is a high-powered offensive team that can win in multiple ways: wire-to-wire blowouts, second-half explosions, and clutch late-game execution. Tavari Johnson is the engine of this offense — a legitimate star who dropped 35 points against Yale, 32 against Western Michigan, 28 against Murray State, and 25 in three separate games, including the MAC Tournament opener against Buffalo. He is the team's primary playmaker and closer, and entering the tournament he is playing at an all-conference level. Amani Lyles is a versatile, rebounding-forward presence who routinely posts double-doubles and provides efficient interior scoring. Shammah Scott is a dangerous three-point shooter who erupted for 25 in the Yale loss and has hit four or more threes in multiple games. Bowen Hardman adds a reliable perimeter option off the bench, shooting 5-for-6 from three against Northern Illinois. This roster has real depth and multiple scoring threats, but the Purdue game exposed a vulnerability: elite size and rebounding can physically overwhelm them, and their defense can leak points in a hurry when opponents get hot from three. Akron plays primarily at home and dominates there, but has shown they can win on the road and at neutral sites when healthy and focused. As a 12-seed with a legitimate star, a hot finish, and a versatile offensive arsenal, **Akron is a genuine first-round upset threat and has the tools to reach the Sweet 16** — but their defensive limitations and lack of wins against top-25 competition make a run beyond that a significant ask.

name: St. John's
summaries:
2025-11-03 - St. John's: 108 vs Quinnipiac: 74

NEW YORK -- — Dillon Mitchell scored 18 points, Zuby Ejiofor added 17 and No. 5 St. John's opened its third season under Hall of Fame coach Rick Pitino by overwhelming Quinnipiac 108-74 on Monday night. Ian Jackson had 15 points on 6-for-6 shooting and Joson Sanon, another touted transfer, added 14 off the bench as the new-look Red Storm racked up their most points since beating Mississippi Valley State 119-61 on Nov. 9, 2021. Coming off a rousing turnaround season that included the program’s first NCAA Tournament victory in 25 years, the reigning Big East champions brought in the nation’s top-rated transfer class to surround Ejiofor, the preseason conference player of the year. All that hype earned the Johnnies the highest preseason ranking in school history. St. John’s showed off its newfound firepower right away, racing to a 19-4 lead 4:40 into the game after a steal and fast-break dunk by Mitchell. Nine players scored in the first 12 minutes, and the margin swelled to 45 with under 5 1/2 minutes remaining. Playing on their Queens campus, the Red Storm never trailed and won their 12th straight season opener. They haven't lost a home opener since 2003-04. Pitino improved to 15-0 at Carnesecca Arena with St. John's, and 30-1 in his last 31 home openers overall. Jaden Zimmerman had 19 points for the Bobcats (0-1), picked to win the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference after taking the past two regular-season titles. Keith Mcknight also scored 19 off the bench. In a pregame ceremony, St. John's showed video highlights of last season and unveiled banners commemorating their Big East regular-season and tournament titles, along with Pitino's national coach of the year award. The four returning players received their conference championship rings, and Pitino addressed the crowd. “We're so proud of what we accomplished,” he said, before turning to the upcoming season: “We're going after the biggest prize of them all.” Up next Quinnipiac: Will host Central Connecticut on Thursday night. St. John's: An early test Saturday against No. 15 Alabama at Madison Square Garden. That's the first of 12 regular-season home games scheduled at MSG — most for the Johnnies since 1951-52. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-08 - St. John's: 96 vs Alabama: 103

NEW YORK -- — Labaron Philon scored 25 points and No. 15 Alabama passed an early season non-conference test with a 103-96 victory over fifth-ranked St. John’s on Saturday afternoon. Philon helped Alabama clinch the win with a deep 3-pointer for a 98-88 lead with two minutes remaining. He shot 10 of 17 from the field in his second straight strong performance as Alabama took control late in the first half, withstood a comeback and executed in the final minutes. Aden Holloway added 21 points after sitting out the season opener with a wrist injury as the Crimson Tide (2-0) shot 48.1%. 2026-03-14 - St. John's: 72 vs UConn: 52

NEW YORK -- — With another St. John's championship salted away, Zuby Ejiofor walked slowly to the sideline with seconds remaining, tears trickling down the senior forward's face as he lowered his 6-foot-9 frame for multiple hugs. Moments later, in a fitting scene dripping with symbolism, he and Hall of Fame coach Rick Pitino climbed a ladder together to cut down the nets at Madison Square Garden. No argument anymore about who owns the Big East these days. St. John's has toppled UConn. Ejiofor had seven blocks, nine rebounds and three steals to anchor a tenacious defensive performance that carried No. 13 St. John's to its second straight Big East Tournament title Saturday night with a 72-52 blowout of sixth-ranked UConn. Ejiofor and Bryce Hopkins each scored 18 points for the top-seeded Red Storm (28-6), who became the first team to win consecutive Big East Tournament titles since Villanova took three in a row from 2017-19. “What a beautiful moment. What a surreal moment," said Ejiofor, who broke the record for blocks in a Big East championship game. “We had a target on our backs all year.” Adding to their resurgent rise under the 73-year-old Pitino, the Johnnies joined UConn in 1998 and '99 as the only Big East programs to win both the regular-season and tournament crowns in back-to-back seasons. It was the fifth Big East Tournament title in school history and the first time the Johnnies have accomplished the feat in consecutive years. They are 11-1 when seeded No. 1 at MSG, one of their home courts during the regular season. “This championship means the world to us. It means the world to our fans,” Pitino said during the postgame celebration on the court. “I'm so proud of every one of our players.” Ejiofor was selected the tournament's Most Outstanding Player, his latest in a string of accolades this week that included Big East Player of the Year, Defensive Player of the Year and Scholar-Athlete of the Year. He's the first player to win all those awards in the same season. Oziyah Sellers scored 14 points for St. John's, which took two of three meetings this season between the two conference powerhouses and turned the tables after an embarrassing 72-40 loss at rival UConn on Feb. 25. “We never mentioned revenge,” said Pitino, whose team has won six straight games and 19 of 20. “We just talked about championship.” The dominant Johnnies never trailed in the tournament, either. Over the past two years, they won their six games in this event by an average of 16 points — all by double digits. In a matchup of the past two Big East Tournament champions, the second-seeded Huskies (29-5) were held nine points below their previous season low. They went the last 8:02 without a field goal, missing 13 straight shots while falling to 1-4 against St. John's the last two seasons. UConn remained tied with Georgetown for the most Big East Tournament championships at eight. “I didn’t crush them in there,” said Dan Hurley, who coached the Huskies to consecutive national championships in 2023 and '24. “They’re crushed. The group is crushed. We laid an egg in something that we desperately wanted to win. I mean, we just laid an egg.” Banging away with Ejiofor down low in their beefy matchup of All-Big East big men, Tarris Reed Jr. scored 13 of his 17 points in the second half for UConn. Point guard Silas Demary Jr. was helped off the floor late with a left ankle injury, which could be costly heading into the NCAA Tournament. Hurley said he initially heard the injury was a very mild sprain and hopes “it’s not something that lingers.” With their fans in a split crowd roaring at The Garden, the Red Storm ran out to a 10-0 lead, built a 17-point cushion late in the first half and went into the break with a 40-27 advantage. It was the largest halftime deficit this season for the Huskies, who committed 11 of their 17 turnovers and shot 36% from the field in the opening 20 minutes. “Huge credit to St. John’s. They just really jumped us to start the game,” Hurley said. “They've got a team that’s built incredibly well to play in this conference." St. John’s extended the margin to 18 early in the second half before the Huskies responded with a 13-2 spurt that trimmed the gap to 49-42 with 12:34 left, revving up their own chanting fans. But that was as close as they got. Thinking his players looked tired, Pitino took a timeout and said he told them: “'Johnnies don't fatigue. Johnnies don't surrender.'” When play resumed, Hopkins hit a jumper and Ejiofor drained a 3-pointer before scoring in the post. Dylan Darling later scored the first six points of a 13-0 surge down the stretch capped by another 3 from Ejiofor, and the Red Storm won going away. "We knew they were going to make their runs. But we knew we had to fight back and that’s exactly what we did,” Ejiofor said. It was the third time UConn and St. John’s squared off for the Big East Tournament title, after splitting matchups in 1999 and 2000. And this marked the first time the championship game featured two of the conference’s seven charter members since the Johnnies beat the Huskies 80-70 in that 2000 game. The hot-tempered Hurley was whistled for a technical foul 7:26 into the game after stamping his feet loudly on the sideline, apparently wanting a foul called as Demary made a reverse layup. St. John’s scored the first eight points in all three tournament games this week and led for more than 118 of 120 minutes. Pitino won his 16th conference tournament title, fifth in the Big East after three with Louisville. He is the first Big East coach to win back-to-back at two schools. “Three straight nights we didn’t relent at all. That’s a great sign offensively and a great sign defensively going into the (NCAA) Tournament,” Pitino said. Up next UConn figures to be a high seed in its sixth straight NCAA Tournament, which will set a program record. St. John’s is headed to consecutive NCAA Tournaments for the first time since making three straight from 1998-2000. It will be Pitino’s 25th appearance, with six schools. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP mobile app). AP college basketball: and
profile_summary: St. John's enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 5-seed with a 28-6 record and back-to-back Big East regular-season and tournament championships — a program that has undergone a genuine transformation under Hall of Fame coach Rick Pitino. Against ranked opponents, the Red Storm went approximately 5-7, with marquee wins over No. 3 UConn (81-72 at MSG), No. 6 UConn again (72-52 in the Big East title game), and No. 15 Baylor (96-81), but also notable losses to No. 15 Alabama, No. 15 Iowa State, No. 21 Auburn, and a brutal 72-40 blowout at UConn in late February that exposed real vulnerabilities when their offense goes cold. Their final five games tell a compelling story of resilience and dominance: a 72-65 road win at Seton Hall to clinch the Big East title, followed by blowout tournament victories over Providence (85-72), Seton Hall (78-68), and UConn (72-52), punctuated by Zuby Ejiofor's triple-double demolition of Villanova (89-57) just before the tournament run. That 13-game winning streak snapped by the UConn road blowout is worth noting — this team can completely disappear offensively, as evidenced by shooting 20% and missing their final 24 field goal attempts at Storrs — but the response, winning six straight and outscoring opponents by an average of roughly 19 points per game through the Big East Tournament, is equally telling about their mental makeup. Ejiofor is the engine: the Big East Player, Defensive Player, and Scholar-Athlete of the Year is producing at an elite level, delivering 21 points, 10 rebounds, and seven blocks in the conference title game alone, and he sets both the physical and emotional tone for this team. Supporting him is a deep, versatile roster — Bryce Hopkins provides 17-20 point outbursts with elite rebounding, Dylan Darling is a clutch bench weapon, Oziyah Sellers is a streaky but dangerous three-point threat, and Dillon Mitchell is a high-energy two-way contributor — though Ian Jackson's sprained ankle bears monitoring heading into the bracket. St. John's plays with genuine two-way identity: they win with paint dominance (54 points in the paint against William & Mary), fast-break efficiency, and a suffocating interior defense anchored by Ejiofor's shot-blocking, but they have also demonstrated a pronounced habit of digging themselves massive second-half holes — trailing by 15 or more against Seton Hall, Xavier, Georgetown, and Providence — before engineering dramatic comebacks, which is both a testament to their toughness and a warning sign against elite tournament opponents who won't let such deficits slip away. Their location splits reinforce their identity as a Madison Square Garden team: Pitino is 15-0 at Carnesecca Arena and the Red Storm have been electric at MSG all season, but they dropped games at Iowa State, Auburn, and Kentucky, and absorbed that catastrophic loss at UConn, suggesting road and neutral environments introduce real inconsistency. Still, the Big East Tournament — played on their home neutral floor at MSG — produced three dominant, wire-to-wire performances, and the program's experience navigating hostile environments during a 6-0 road stretch in conference play adds credibility to their neutral-site résumé. **Verdict**: St. John's is a legitimate Elite Eight threat and cannot be dismissed as a Final Four contender. Their combination of a dominant star in Ejiofor, proven clutch scoring, championship-level recent form, and big-game experience against UConn specifically gives them the profile of a team capable of advancing deep — but their tendency to vanish offensively for long stretches and their 5-7 record against ranked opponents signals they are not yet a lock-step national title contender. Expect them to win their first two rounds comfortably and threaten seriously in the regional, with advancement to the Final Four within genuine reach if Ejiofor remains healthy and the offense doesn't go cold at the wrong moment.

2026-03-08 - UIC: 69 vs Northern Iowa: 84
profile_summary: Northern Iowa enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 12-seed with a 23-12 record — a résumé built on Missouri Valley Conference toughness rather than marquee non-conference scalps. The Panthers' best non-conference wins came against South Dakota State and Oakland, but they dropped games to Tulsa, Wichita State, and Saint Mary's (63-58, in a game where they shot just 3-of-5 from the free-throw line against a team that went 25-of-27 from the stripe), suggesting the offense can go flat against disciplined, high-level competition. Against quality MVC opponents, Northern Iowa went through a brutal mid-season stretch — losing five straight at one point — before righting the ship. Their conference tournament run, however, is the most compelling data point on this roster: the Panthers knocked off Illinois State (74-52), upset No. 2 seed Bradley 73-69 on Leon Bond III's 19-point effort (13 in the first half alone), and then blew out UIC 84-69 in the championship, winning the automatic bid as a No. 6 conference seed. That is a genuinely impressive three-day gauntlet. The final five games — wins over Drake (75-53), Evansville (68-59), Illinois State (74-52), Bradley (73-69), and UIC (84-69) — represent a five-game winning streak with an average margin of roughly 17 points, the hottest stretch Northern Iowa has played all season. Trey Campbell is the engine of this offense, and he is playing his best basketball right now: 23 points against Evansville in the tournament opener, 13 points with six assists and four steals against Bradley, and consistent double-digit scoring throughout the stretch run. Ben Schwieger provides a reliable second option (17 points, 10 rebounds vs. Bradley in Game 17; 15 points vs. Illinois State in Game 30), and Leon Bond III can take over in bursts. Will Hornseth adds toughness as a two-way contributor and rebounder. The Panthers play a grind-it-out, defensively physical style — they held Bradley to 69 and UIC to 69 in back-to-back tournament games — but they are capable of eruptions, as seen in their 89-60 demolition of Murray State in February. Location-wise, Northern Iowa appears comfortable on neutral sites: they won the MVC Tournament in St. Louis and performed respectably in early-season neutral-site games (splitting at the Acrisure Holiday Invitational), though they have shown vulnerability on the road, including losses at Southern Illinois, Valparaiso, and Illinois State. Their lone real flaw heading in is free-throw shooting and a tendency to go cold offensively in bursts — they were held to 44 points at Valparaiso and 50 at Southern Illinois in January — which remains a legitimate concern against NCAA Tournament-caliber defense. **Verdict**: Northern Iowa is a genuinely dangerous 12-seed riding a five-game hot streak with a battle-tested backcourt, a recent upset win over a quality MVC opponent in Bradley, and a clear identity. They are a realistic first-round upset threat and have the pieces to reach the Sweet 16, but their inconsistency against elite competition and offensive fragility in road-like environments caps their realistic ceiling at **a second-round appearance, with a Sweet 16 run possible if Campbell continues his current form**.

2025-11-04 - Tennessee: 76 vs Mercer: 61

KNOXVILLE, Tenn. -- — Nate Ament scored 18 points and had nine rebounds to lead No. 18 Tennessee to a 76-61 victory over Mercer on Monday night in the season opener for both teams Ament, regarded as one of the top freshmen in the country, was much more efficient shooting against Mercer (6 of 11) than he was in last week's exhibition loss to Duke (5 of 19). J.P. Estrella chipped in 12 with points and Jaylen Carey had 10 rebounds. The Bears were led by Baraka Okojie with 15 points, Armani Mighty with 14 and Zaire Williams with 10. Tennessee, which is coming off back-to-back Elite Eight appearances under coach Rick Barnes, struggled with possession. The Vols had 19 turnovers to just five for Mercer. Tennessee, however, made up for that by dominating the boards 54-32. Mercer, which was 14-19 last year under coach Ryan Ridder, shot just 30% (20 for 67). Tennessee led by 23 points in the second half, but the Bears closed the gap to 13 at one point. Both teams labored through a sluggish first half. Tennessee used a 13-2 run midway to get some breathing room, 17-10. The Volunteers turned the ball over 11 times in the first 20 minutes but still led 33-21 at halftime. Mercer shot just 21% (7 of 33). Estrella had eight points at the break for Tennessee. Up next Mercer: The Bears plays host to LaGrange on Wednesday night. Tennessee: The Vols take on Northern Kentucky on Saturday. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). LAS VEGAS -- — Ja'Kobi Gillespie scored 22 points and made all six of his free throws in the final 35 seconds to lift No. 17 Tennessee to a 76-73 victory over No. 3 Houston in the Players Era Championship on Tuesday. Jaylen Carey added 13 points for the Volunteers (7-0) and Bishop Boswell scored 10, including two free throws with 25 seconds left. Kingston Flemings led Houston (6-1) with 25 points, and Milos Uzan had 17. Uzan, playing in his hometown, missed a halfcourt shot at the buzzer that would have sent the game into overtime. Houston guard Emanuel Sharp, who averages 17.7 points per game, was limited to 21 minutes because of foul trouble. He finished with eight points. Sharp, who scored 26 in Monday’s 78-74 overtime victory against Syracuse, picked up two fouls in the first five minutes and sat the rest of the half. These teams met in last season's NCAA Tournament with a Final Four trip on the line. Houston won the Elite Eight game 69-50, eventually advancing to the championship before losing in the final minute to Florida. The rematch was quite different. Houston couldn't get much to go down in the second half, enabling Tennessee to go on a 10-point run for a 51-46 lead with 9:42 left. The Cougars went 7:24 between points and 7:42 between field goals, and they missed 11 straight shots and 16 of 18. Tennessee remained in front the rest of the way, though the Cougars kept the game close. But the Volunteers held off Houston by converting free throws. LAS VEGAS -- — Melvin Council Jr. and Elmarko Jackson each scored 17 points and Kansas defeated previously unbeaten No. 17 Tennessee 81-76 in the third-place game of the Players Era Championship on Wednesday night. Tre White added 14 points for Kansas (6-2) before fouling out and Florey Bidunga scored 13. Nata Ament led Tennessee (7-1) with 20 points. Jaylen Carey and Ja’Kobe Gillespie each scored 11 points and J.P. Estrella added 10. Kansas, which trailed most of the second half, took the lead with 5:36 remaining on Paul Mbiya's layup and extended the lead to four points at 68-64 on a driving layup by Jackson with 5:08 left. The Jayhawks extended the lead to seven points at 75-68 with 2:02 left before Tennessee cut the lead to 75-73 with 56 seconds left. profile_summary:
Tennessee enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 6-seed with a 22-11 record — a résumé defined by genuine high-end wins, chronic inconsistency, and a troubling late-season swoon. Against ranked opponents, the Vols went 7-10: notable victories include a 76-73 overtime thriller over then-No. 3 Houston in the Players Era tournament, a road upset of No. 17 Alabama, a come-from-behind home win over No. 11 Louisville, and a grind-out road win at No. 19 Vanderbilt — but losses to Kansas, Syracuse, Illinois, Florida (by 24), Kentucky twice, Missouri, Alabama, and Vanderbilt twice illustrate a team that cannot consistently close the deal against elite competition. Over their final five games — a narrow 4-point win at Vanderbilt, a two-point loss at Missouri, a two-point home loss to Alabama (with star freshman Nate Ament exiting injured), a 19-point road win at South Carolina (without Ament), and back-to-back SEC Tournament losses to Vanderbilt — the Vols went 2-3, and the pattern of squandering double-digit leads has been a persistent and damaging theme: they surrendered 17-point halftime leads against both Kentucky and Alabama in the second half, costing them games they had well in hand. Tennessee's style is built around interior dominance — they consistently win the rebounding battle by large margins (52-27 in one Georgia game, 60-35 in double-overtime against Texas A&M) and attack the paint aggressively, but perimeter inconsistency and turnover problems (19 in the season opener, 18 against Florida) have undermined them all year.

The offense runs through two centerpieces: junior guard Ja'Kobi Gillespie, who provides elite shot-creation and timely shot-making — his 32-point, six-3-pointer explosion against Rutgers and game-saving free throws against Houston define his ceiling — and freshman Nate Ament, a 6-foot-10 playmaker averaging over 20 points in SEC play who has delivered multiple 27-29 point performances but is entering the tournament managing a right knee and high ankle sprain that limited him to 12 points on 4-of-12 shooting in the SEC Tournament loss to Vanderbilt after missing two full games. Supporting contributors J.P. Estrella (22 points and 20 points in back-to-back late-season games), Felix Okpara (18 points and five dunks against Oklahoma), and sophomore Bishop Boswell provide reliable depth, but this team lives and dies by Ament and Gillespie. The Vols are clearly a stronger home team — their 42-game nonconference home win streak and dominant victories over Louisville and Texas A&M at Thompson-Boling Arena contrast sharply with road struggles at Florida, Arkansas, Missouri, and Kentucky — though they showed neutral-site quality with the Houston win in Las Vegas. The energy reads as a team capable of explosive, confident performances but equally capable of going cold, turning it over in bunches, and losing games they have firmly in their grasp; the back-to-back late-season collapse losses to Alabama and Vanderbilt, combined with Ament's injury uncertainty, are legitimate red flags. Tennessee is a dangerous, tournament-tested team with wins over genuine top-15 competition, but their realistic ceiling is the **Sweet 16** — healthy enough to beat a 3- or 11-seed on talent and rebounding alone, but too prone to second-half collapses and too dependent on a dinged-up freshman to project a deeper run against the elite competition the back half of the bracket will bring.

The 73-year-old Hall of Famer also got a chance to go against Kentucky coach Mark Pope, who was a captain on Pitino's championship team in Lexington. Quaintance, a touted transfer from Arizona State, made his Kentucky debut nine month after tearing a knee ligament. He looked full recovered, finishing with 10 points, eight rebounds and two blocks. The pro-Kentucky crowd in Atlanta — dubbed “Cat-Lanta” for the Wildcats' long-time success in the city — erupted when Quaintance headed to the scorer's table five minutes into the game. He drew another huge cheer when he banked in his first shot, and yet another when he swatted away a shot by Sellers. 2026-03-13 - Florida: 71 vs Kentucky: 63

NASHVILLE, Tenn. -- — Alex Condon scored 22 points and grabbed 10 rebounds, and fourth-ranked Florida took the first step toward defending its Southeastern Conference Tournament title by beating Kentucky 71-63 Friday in the quarterfinals. The Gators (26-6) never trailed and were tied only once while winning their 12th straight game. The regular-season SEC champions, who are seeking a sixth tournament title overall, will play either No. 22 Vanderbilt or No. 25 Tennessee in Saturday's semifinals. Thomas Haugh added 13 points for Florida, and Xaivian Lee had 11. Kentucky (21-13) now has one more loss all-time (32) than titles (31) in this tournament. The ninth-seeded Wildcats played their third game in as many days after opening the tournament Wednesday morning, a first for a program that last won this event in 2018. Denzel Aberdeen led Kentucky with 17 points, reserve Mouhamed Dioubate scored 14 and Otega Oweh added 10. During the Gators' streak, Kentucky was the only team to lose within single digits and did it twice, including in the regular-season finale at Rupp Arena. Florida was up 16-6 quickly on a turnaround jumper by Condon with 14:13 left. Kentucky tied it up at 20. Boogie Fland then started a 13-point run with consecutive jumpers in a spurt that included a one-handed putback slam by Micah Handlogten. Fland ended the run with a 3-pointer, and Florida took a 37-28 lead at halftime. The Gators led by as much as 17 before missing 12 of 13 shots in the second. Kentucky got within 66-61 on a 3-pointer by Aberdeen with 1:19 left. Lee hit a deep 3 from the top of the arc with 50.5 seconds remaining, then stole the ball from Aberdeen to preserve the win. Up next Kentucky returns home to rest and wait for its seeding and NCAA Tournament destination. Florida takes another step toward defending its tournament championship. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). profile_summary:
Kentucky enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 7-seed with a 21-13 record — a résumé built on resilience and star power, but undermined by persistent inconsistency and a brutal injury-shortened season. Against ranked opponents, the Wildcats went 6-12, with notable wins over No. 25 Tennessee (twice), No. 22 St. John's, No. 15 Arkansas, and No. 24 Tennessee on the road, but suffered blowout losses to No. 11 Gonzaga (94-59), No. 17 Michigan State (83-66), and No. 5 Florida twice, as well as home losses to North Carolina, Missouri, and Georgia — programs Kentucky should have handled at Rupp Arena. The injury toll was significant all season: starting point guard Jaland Lowe dislocated his shoulder in preseason and ultimately underwent season-ending surgery in January, Jayden Quaintance (knee) missed large stretches after his much-anticipated debut, and Jamarion Davis-Fleming was also lost mid-season, forcing coach Mark Pope to constantly reconfigure his rotation. Entering the tournament, Kentucky's final five games paint a mixed picture — wins over Vanderbilt (91-77) and Missouri in the SEC Tournament were encouraging, but the Wildcats also dropped to Texas A&M (96-85) in a second-half collapse, fell to Florida 84-77 in the regular-season finale, and were then dispatched 71-63 by Florida in the SEC quarterfinals after playing three games in three days, finishing that stretch looking fatigued and undermanned.

The engine of this Kentucky team is unambiguously Otega Oweh, the preseason SEC Player of the Year, who has scored in double figures in every game this season and posted a career-high 29 points against Auburn; without him, this team has no consistent offensive identity. Collin Chandler and Denzel Aberdeen provide secondary firepower, with Aberdeen posting a memorable 22-point second-half performance at Tennessee and Chandler hitting a career-high 23 in the Vanderbilt rematch, but both have been maddeningly inconsistent — particularly from three-point range. Kentucky's style is defined by clutch comebacks (erasing 17-point deficits at both Tennessee and LSU in the same week) and free-throw-line dependence, rather than dominant wire-to-wire performances; they are collapse-prone in the first half but capable of historic second-half surges. Defensively, the Wildcats can generate steals and force turnovers in bunches — 14 steals against Mississippi State stands out — but they have been gashed by elite perimeter shooting and routinely outrebounded by upper-tier opponents. At home, Kentucky went 11-4, but their 10-9 mark away from Rupp Arena and neutral sites reveals a team that struggles to sustain energy on the road, with road blowout losses to Gonzaga and Michigan State exposing their ceiling against elite competition. The tournament ceiling for this Kentucky team is realistically the Round of 32: Oweh's star power and their proven capacity for dramatic comebacks make them a dangerous first-round opponent, but the loss of Lowe, the inability to contain elite perimeter offenses, chronic first-half struggles, and a 6-12 record against ranked teams all point to a team unlikely to advance past a well-coached, healthy opponent in the second round.

2026-03-11 - Gonzaga: 79 vs Santa Clara: 68

LAS VEGAS -- — Gonzaga has dominated the West Coast Conference since coach Mark Few took over at the turn of the century. The Bulldogs are leaving for the Pac-12 Conference next season and made sure to go out winners. Mario Saint-Supéry scored 21 points, Tyon Grant-Foster had 20 and No. 12 Gonzaga defeated Santa Clara 79-68 on Tuesday night to win the WCC Tournament. “It's a special feeling to go out the right way,” said Gonzaga's Graham Ike, voted the tournament Most Outstanding Player. “Ultimately, we ended where we started this. It started out with championships and those teams that came before us. We just wanted to continue the success that they had. We were proud we could do that.” The Bulldogs (30-3) received the conference's automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament, though the only mystery was what seed they will get. This was their sixth WCC tournament title in seven years and 12th in the last 14. Santa Clara (26-8) will wait until Sunday to see if it is in the 68-team field, but the WCC appears to be a three-bid league. Gonzaga, Santa Clara and No. 21 Saint Mary's were in the top 38 of the NCAA's NET rankings entering this game and in the top 35 in Kenpom. Four players scored in double figures for the Zags, who made 52.8% of their shots. Ike made all seven of his shots in scoring 15 points. Christian Hammond led the Broncos with 24 points, and Allen Graves scored 11. Sash Gavalyugov, who scored 23 points that included the dagger 3-pointer in the closing seconds to beat Saint Mary's 76-71 in the semifinals, was held to eight. He was 3 of 13, including 2 of 9 from 3-point range. “Sash had some tough shots tonight,” Santa Clara coach Herb Sendek said. “He does have the ability to make some of those shots, but I don't know that things were engineered to simply take him out.” The Broncos made Gonzaga earn this victory. They led 52-50 nearly midway through the second half before the Bulldogs scored seven straight points to go ahead for good. After dominating the WCC, Gonzaga will see if it can continue to assert itself against San Diego State, Boise State and Utah State in the revamped Pac-12 beginning next season. That will come later. Gonzaga has yet another NCAA Tournament appearance to place its focus. “After like 2015, we really focused on NCAA Tournament wins and Final Fours and things like that,” Few said. “The national championship's going to be the standard that we're aiming for. But we've always talked about taking care of business along the way so that you can get to the prize at the end, which is the NCAA Tournament. These guys have done a good job of keeping their eyes on the prize.” ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). profile_summary:
Santa Clara enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 10-seed with a 26-8 record, having built one of the more compelling mid-major résumés in the country after winning the WCC regular-season title and reaching the conference championship game. Against quality competition, the Broncos acquitted themselves well in spots — most notably a dominant 87-68 neutral-site win over Xavier in November and a 96-92 home win over Washington State — but their profile against elite opponents reveals real limitations. In ranked games, Santa Clara went 0-3: a 12-point loss at No. 8 Gonzaga in January, a 8-point home loss to No. 12 Gonzaga in February, and an 11-point loss to the Bulldogs again in the WCC Championship game. That said, they pushed Gonzaga to within two points midway through the second half of that title game before the Bulldogs pulled away, which is meaningful context. The five-game stretch to close the season tells a nuanced story: a 19-point win over San Francisco, a blowout loss at Saint Mary's (86-67), a comfortable regular-season-finale win over Oregon State, a WCC quarterfinal win over Pacific, a dramatic semifinal win over No. 21 Saint Mary's (76-71), and a WCC final loss to Gonzaga (79-68) — making their recent form a mixed bag of dominant performances punctuated by lapses against top-end competition.

Stylistically, Santa Clara is a high-octane offensive team that can fill it up from multiple sources, averaging well over 80 points in most wins and demonstrating the ability to build large halftime leads — 53-20 over Oregon State, 44-31 over Loyola Marymount — but they have shown a troubling tendency to let big advantages evaporate, as illustrated by the Arizona State collapse where they surrendered a 19-point second-half lead. The key players are legitimately dangerous: Christian Hammond is a multi-level scorer who poured in 24 points in the WCC final, Elijah Mahi is a reliable 17-22 point performer who can take over games (30 points at San Francisco), Allen Graves provides elite two-way production off the bench, and Sash Gavalyugov is a wild-card weapon capable of 23-point explosions — though he was held to 8 points on 3-of-13 shooting in the championship loss, a reminder of his inconsistency. The Broncos appear confident and resilient, capable of thrilling comebacks — the Portland road win, erasing a 10-2 deficit against Saint Mary's in the semifinal — but they are also collapse-prone when opponents make extended runs, and their three losses to Gonzaga confirm they struggle to close out elite opponents. On location, they are a strong home team (the Leavey Center crowd was a factor against Gonzaga in February) but have shown they can compete on the road and neutral sites, with neutral-site wins over Xavier and Minnesota and a gutty semifinal win over Saint Mary's in Las Vegas. Santa Clara's realistic tournament ceiling is the Sweet 16: they are dangerous enough to beat a 7-seed and potentially a 2-seed on a good night given their offensive firepower and multiple reliable scorers, but their inability to close out Gonzaga-caliber opponents three times this season and their vulnerability to second-half collapses make a run deeper than the Sweet 16 unlikely.

The Cardinals clawed back, cutting the lead to one with 1:23 left when Hadley came up with a steal and Issac McKneely put back Hadley's miss off a fastbreak layup. But Reneau made a layup and Washington came up with the play of the game when he stole the ensuing inbounds pass and was fouled. He made both free throws to push the lead to five with less than a minute to play. Reneau added two free throws and Shelton Henderson sealed the win with a steal and powerful two-handed dunk. Louisville finished 6 of 24 from 3-point range. Up next Louisville: Awaits its NCAA Tournament bid. 2026-03-13 - Virginia: 84 vs Miami: 62

CHARLOTTE, N.C. -- — Ugonna Onyenso scored 17 points on 8-for-9 shooting, Thijs De Ridder and Sam Lewis each scored 16 points and No. 10 Virginia beat Miami 84-62 on Friday to advance to the ACC Tournament championship game for the first time since 2023. Virginia will face No. 1 Duke, a 73-61 semifinal winner over Clemson, in the title game on Saturday. Tru Washington scored 13 points and Shelton Henderson added 12 for Miami, which shot just 39% from the field and was outrebounded 35-24. Virginia broke open a tightly contested defensive game with a 13-2 run over the final 2:47 of the first half for a 38-23 lead at the break. Chance Mallory closed out the run with a running 3-pointer to beat the buzzer following a Miami turnover. The Hurricanes never recovered. The lead grew to 18 points six minutes into the second half as Miami struggled to find lanes to attack the basket against the taller Cavaliers. Miami couldn't hit from deep, missing 10 of its first 11 shots from behind the 3-point line. Miami made a run and closed within 53-42 after Noam Dovrat hit two 3s and Henderson followed up a miss on a fastbreak with a two-handed dunk. But Thomas took over and scored eight of Virginia's next 10 points to push the lead back to 18 points with eight minutes left. Virginia's lead reached 26 points late in the second half. profile_summary: Miami enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 7-seed with a 25-8 record — a résumé that represents a remarkable 17-win improvement over last season under first-year coach Jai Lucas and includes legitimate victories against quality ACC competition. Against ranked opponents, the Hurricanes went approximately 4-7: notable wins include a road victory at Ole Miss in the ACC/SEC Challenge, a home win over #14 North Carolina, a road win over #22 Clemson-era-adjacent NC State in a dramatic comeback, and a tournament rematch win over #24 Louisville in the ACC quarterfinals; losses came to #10 Florida, #9 BYU, #22 Clemson (held to a season-low 59 points), #14 Virginia (twice, including an ugly 84-62 ACC semifinal blowout), and #22-ranked Louisville in the regular season finale. The final five games paint a mixed picture: Miami won four of five, including road wins at SMU and a tournament win over Louisville, but sandwiched around those were a narrow 92-89 home loss to Louisville and a 22-point ACC semifinal blowout at the hands of Virginia — a team that exposed Miami's size disadvantages and three-point shooting inconsistency when the Cavaliers went on a 13-2 closing run and the Hurricanes shot just 39% while missing 10 of their first 11 from deep. Miami's identity is built on offensive firepower and clutch execution rather than defensive dominance — they regularly score in the 75-90 point range, lead the ACC in field-goal percentage, and have demonstrated a habit of dramatic comebacks, including erasing a 10-point halftime deficit against Pittsburgh, scoring the final eight points to beat NC State from seven down with a minute to play, and surviving multiple single-possession thrillers. The engine of this team is Malik Reneau (Indiana transfer), who is averaging around 20 points on efficient shooting, capable of carrying the offense late — his 28-point, 10-free-throw performance against Pitt and 26-point comeback effort at NC State define his value — though he has fouled out in multiple key games, which is a genuine tournament vulnerability. Tre Donaldson (Michigan transfer) is arguably equally important as a shot-creator and facilitator who can erupt for 27-32 points on any given night, while Shelton Henderson provides consistent two-way production and Tru Washington offers a reliable third scoring option when healthy; the team also suffered the loss of sophomore Marcus Allen to non-Hodgkin lymphoma mid-season, a sobering human element to this team's story. Miami is notably stronger at home (dominant in Coral Gables all season) and has performed respectably on neutral sites, going 1-1 in the ESPN Events Invitational and 1-1 in the ACC Tournament, but they have struggled on true road trips — losing at Clemson, Florida State, Virginia, and nearly dropping games at Boston College and Wake Forest. The team projects as confident and resilient in close games but is prone to flat stretches, three-point shooting volatility (as low as 1-of-14 in single games), and foul trouble derailing their best player at critical moments. Miami's realistic tournament ceiling is the Sweet 16: they are dangerous enough to knock off a 10-seed in the first round and potentially steal a second-round win with Donaldson or Reneau erupting, but the Virginia blowout — combined with their inconsistent perimeter shooting and Reneau's foul trouble pattern — suggests they lack the defensive structure and elite-level depth to consistently beat top-25 caliber teams when the margin for error disappears.
