profile_summary:
Missouri enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 10-seed with a 20-12 record — a résumé that reflects genuine mid-tier quality in the SEC, though one riddled with the kind of inconsistency that makes projection difficult. Against ranked opponents, the Tigers went an impressive 5-7, with notable wins over #22 Florida (76-74 at home), #22 Tennessee (73-69 at home), and #19 Vanderbilt (81-80 at home), as well as a road stunner at Kentucky (73-68) that marked Missouri's first-ever win at Rupp Arena. However, the losses are equally instructive: blowout defeats to #20 Illinois (91-48) and #23 Alabama (90-64) reveal a team that can be thoroughly dismantled by elite competition, and consecutive overtime and late-game losses to #20 Arkansas in the regular-season finale compounded concerns heading into the postseason. Their final five games tell a mixed story — a blowout win at Mississippi State (88-64), a narrow home win over Vanderbilt (81-80), a road loss at Arkansas (94-86), an overtime home loss to Arkansas again (88-84), and a second-round SEC Tournament exit to Kentucky (78-72) in which Missouri squandered a late lead — producing a 2-3 stretch that signals a team trending downward rather than peaking at the right moment. Location matters significantly for this Tigers squad: Missouri was dominant at home, going 9-0 before their first home loss to Georgia and finishing with a strong home record, but they struggled considerably on the road, losing at Ole Miss, LSU, Alabama, Arkansas (twice), Oklahoma, and in Lexington before that historic Rupp Arena breakthrough, suggesting the neutral-site tournament environment could neutralize one of their clearest advantages.

The engine of this Missouri team is Mark Mitchell, who is playing at an All-SEC level — his 32-point performance against Arkansas in the regular-season finale, his career-best 32 against Kentucky in the SEC Tournament, and his late-game clutch moments (including a buzzer-beating three to beat Oklahoma 88-87) mark him as a legitimate star capable of carrying the Tigers on any given night. Jayden Stone has been a reliable secondary scorer and playmaker, and T.O. Barrett has emerged as a high-energy starter — his career-high 28-point performance against Tennessee was a season-defining moment. Jacob Crews provides shootout range as a stretch scorer, and Shawn Phillips Jr. offers interior physicality, as evidenced by his game-sealing block and alley-oop dunk to win at Texas A&M. However, Missouri's style is volatile rather than disciplined: they are capable of wire-to-wire blowouts and dramatic comebacks in the same week, but they also collapse under sustained pressure, shoot inconsistently from three and the free-throw line (a ghastly 35% from the line in the Alabama loss), and give up 18 offensive rebounds in single games. They thrive in high-scoring environments — 18-1 when scoring 73 or more — but have been blanked in blowouts when opponents control pace and physicality. Missouri's tournament ceiling is a second-round appearance: Mitchell's star power and demonstrated clutch ability make them a credible upset threat in the first round, but their defensive fragility, road-game struggles on neutral floors against elite athletes, and a team that is entering the bracket having lost three of their last four make a Sweet 16 run a significant overachievement rather than a reasonable expectation.

summaries:
2025-11-04 - UCLA: 80 vs Eastern Washington: 74

LOS ANGELES -- — Senior guard Donovan Dent had 21 points and nine assists in his UCLA debut to lead the No. 12 Bruins to an 80-74 victory over Eastern Washington in a season opener Monday night. Tyler Bilodeau scored 19 points for the Bruins, who opened a 15-point lead midway through the second half before the Eagles of the Big Sky Conference made it close in the final minutes. Dent, a heralded transfer from New Mexico, had a memorable performance in his Southern California homecoming. He played the entire first half, scoring 14 points on 6-of-11 shooting, with six assists, to lead UCLA to a 40-33 advantage. His first points in a Bruins uniform came on consecutive possessions in the opening minutes, when he hit a pullup jumper and then a layup. The Eagles led 22-21 when Jamar Brown hit a jumper in the key followed by Dent's 3-point play to give UCLA a 26-22 lead. Dent spent his first three seasons at New Mexico before transferring to UCLA. He played at Centennial High in Corona and got to know UCLA coach Mick Cronin, a relationship that helped him realize his dream of playing at Pauley Pavilion. He was the Mountain West Player of the Year last season after averaging 20.4 points and 6.4 assists and leading the Lobos to the second round of the NCAA Tournament, where New Mexico beat Marquette before losing to Michigan State. Dent was a first-team All-Big Ten preseason pick. Xavier Booker, a transfer from Michigan State, scored 14 points for the Bruins. Trent Perry had 11 and Skyy Clark 10. Isaiah Moses had 15 points and Alton Hamilton IV 13 for Eastern Washington Up next Eastern Washington: At Loyola Marymount on Wednesday night. UCLA: Hosts Pepperdine on Friday night. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here and here (AP News mobile app). AP college basketball: and

2025-11-08 - UCLA: 74 vs Pepperdine: 63

LOS ANGELES -- — Xavier Booker scored 15 points to lead four players in double figures and No. 12 UCLA pulled away in the second half to beat Pepperdine 74-63 on Friday night. Booker made 5 of 8 from the field, 4 of 4 from the free-throw line and had five blocks. Donovan Dent added 12 points and Skyy Clark had 11. Eric Dailey Jr. had 10 points in his season debut after tweaking his knee in October and missing the season opener Monday. The Bruins (2-0) ran off 15 straight points to take an 11-point lead, their largest of the first half. They were outscored 13-11 while closing the half ahead 37-28. Aaron Clark scored 18 points, including 14 of their final 16, to lead the Waves (1-1). Javon Cooley had 17 points and nine rebounds. Trent Perry scored six straight points to give UCLA its largest lead of the game, 68-49. The Waves cut it down on Clark's outburst and a 3-pointer by Cooley. The game brought together three former Bruins — Pepperdine coach Ed Schilling (one-time UCLA assistant), Pepperdine assistant Tyus Edney and Jim Harrick, who coached UCLA to its record 11th national championship in 1995 when Edney starred on the team. Harrick also previously coached the Waves. Missouri-Kansas City transfer Jamar Brown finished with six points and five rebounds for the Bruins, going over the 1,000-point mark in his career. The fifth-year senior spent the last two seasons at UMKC. Up next Pepperdine hosts Lincoln on Tuesday in the first of four straight home games. Bilodeau shot 7 of 12 from the field and made all six of his free throws. Trent Perry added 17 points, nine assists and five rebounds, and Eric Dailey Jr. had 14 points. The Wolves shot 13 of 25 from 3-point range, led by Brady Hardewig with four. The Wolves' first five made baskets were 3-pointers around an 11-0 spurt by the Bruins. From there, the Wolves outscored UCLA 23-17 to trail 37-32 at halftime. Perry scored five of UCLA's first seven points in the second half, extending the lead to 44-34. The Wolves hit three 3s in a row — two by Chas Lewless — to close within five. That's when UCLA scored 10 in a row, including five by Bilodeau and a 3-pointer from Jamar Brown, for a 58-43 lead. The Bruins picked up their shooting in the second half, making 15 of 25 from the floor. The Wolves cooled way down, going 4 of 12 on 3-pointers and 11 of 26 from the floor in the second half. 2025-11-15 - UCLA: 65 vs Arizona: 69

INGLEWOOD, Calif. -- — Anthony Dell'Orso scored 20 points off the bench and Jaden Bradley hit the go-ahead 3-pointer to help No. 5 Arizona rally for a 69-65 victory over 15th-ranked UCLA on Friday night in the Hall of Fame Series at Intuit Dome. The early season neutral-court matchup reunited the longtime Pac-12 Conference rivals, who went their separate ways last season. The Bruins joined the Big Ten, while the Wildcats went to the Big 12. There were dueling chants of “UCLA” and “UofA” from the crowd that created a postseason atmosphere to match the high level of play in the NBA arena. Dell’Orso had 13 points in the first half, when he made three 3-pointers. He was limited to a pair of free throws in the second half until hitting a 3 that put Arizona ahead 60-59. Dell'Orso then sank two free throws with 17 seconds left to give the Wildcats a four-point lead. Tyler Bilodeau scored 19 points on 7-of-10 shooting from the floor for the Bruins (3-1). Eric Dailey Jr. added 13 points and Donovan Dent had 11 points and eight assists. The Bruins opened an eight-point lead behind Bilodeau, the big man who has been playing on the perimeter more this season. His 3-pointer gave UCLA a 57-49 advantage. Arizona then went on an 11-2 run, capped by Dell'Orso's 3. Bradley scored inside, hit a 3-pointer and drove inside again to extend Arizona's lead to 67-63. He finished with 15 points. Last season, the Wildcats reached the Sweet 16 of the NCAA Tournament, while the Bruins were beaten by Tennessee in the second round. The two schools combined to win or share 29 of the final 42 Pac-12 Conference regular-season titles from 1983 through 2024. It was the 115th meeting between the programs. UCLA leads the series 64-51. USC routed Illinois State 87-67 in the opening game of the doubleheader. Up next Arizona visits No. 3 UConn on Wednesday. LOS ANGELES -- — Eric Dailey Jr. had 15 points and 12 rebounds, Xavier Booker scored 12 and No. 19 UCLA rode a new-look starting lineup to a 79-48 victory over Sacramento State on Tuesday night. Trent Perry, Jamar Brown, Steven Jamerson II and Brandon Williams all started for the Bruins (4-1) after making just two starts between them in the first four games of the season. Booker, Tyler Bilodeau, Donovan Dent and Skyy Clark all began the game on the bench. profile_summary: UCLA enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 7-seed with a 23-11 record — a résumé built on genuine toughness, dramatic moments, and a roster that has been significantly compromised heading into March. Against ranked opponents, the Bruins went 4-9: wins over No. 4 Purdue (69-67, walk-off Bilodeau 3-pointer), No. 10 Illinois (95-94 OT comeback from 23 down), No. 9 Nebraska (72-52), and No. 8 Michigan State in the Big Ten Tournament (88-84), offset by losses to Arizona, Gonzaga, Iowa, Wisconsin, Michigan (a 30-point blowout), Michigan State in the regular season (a 23-point blowout), and Purdue in the Big Ten semis. Over the final five games, UCLA went 3-2: a nail-biting OT win over Illinois, a dominant 81-62 win over USC, a road loss at Minnesota (78-73), a home rout of No. 9 Nebraska (72-52), and a blowout of USC again (89-68) to close the regular season — a sequence that shows real punch at home but persistent vulnerability away from Pauley Pavilion, where the Bruins finished an excellent 17-1 compared to a far more modest 4-8 on the road. The team's identity is equal parts clutch resilience and fragility: they have stormed back from massive deficits multiple times (23 down against Illinois, 10 down late against Purdue), but have also suffered consecutive 20-plus-point road blowouts and have a pronounced tendency to let leads evaporate, as seen in the near-collapse at Washington (led by 16 with five minutes left before barely surviving 82-80). The engine of this team is senior guard Donovan Dent, who has been extraordinary down the stretch — recording a triple-double in the Big Ten Tournament opener, posting 23 points and 12 assists in the Michigan State quarterfinal win, and averaging over 12 assists across his final three regular-season games — but he is now compromised after straining his calf in the semifinal loss to Purdue and was limited to two points in 10 minutes in that game. More damaging still is the status of Tyler Bilodeau, the Bruins' leading scorer at 17.9 points per game, who suffered a non-contact right knee injury late in the Michigan State win and did not return for the Purdue semifinal; his availability for the NCAA Tournament is uncertain, and without him UCLA loses its most reliable scorer (seven 20-point games, including a 34-point career-high against UC Riverside) and a player capable of taking over games. When healthy, this is a legitimately dangerous team with five contributors who can reach 20 points on a given night — Perry, Clark, Dailey Jr., Booker, and Dent joining Bilodeau — and a coaching staff under Mick Cronin that has proven capable of tactical adjustments, including mid-season lineup overhauls and legendary second-half defensive efforts. The Bruins' tournament ceiling, however, hinges almost entirely on health: a fully healthy UCLA squad with Bilodeau and Dent operating normally has the clutch pedigree, the star power, and the big-game experience to reach the Sweet 16 and potentially push into the Elite Eight as a dangerous 7-seed — but with Bilodeau's knee in question and Dent managing a calf strain, this is a team that could just as easily fall in the first round, making them one of the most volatile and unpredictable teams in the bracket.

The Knights won despite going 3 of 24 from beyond the 3-point arc. Cincinnati had taken control with about 10 minutes to go, when Thomas was fouled by Kugel about 30 feet from the basket and the shot-clock running out. His two free throws gave the Bearcats a 46-37 lead, and Stillwell immediately picked up his fourth foul at the other end, sending the Knights' best rebounder to the bench. UCF still faced a 58-50 hole with about two minutes to go when Stillwell hit a soft jumper in the lane. Cincinnati proceeded to turn the ball over on three straight possessions, and Kugel's driving layup tied the game 58-all with a minute to go. The Bearcats had the final shot of regulation, but Thiam didn't appear to realize the clock was about to expire. Cincinnati coach Wes Miller frantically called timeout from the bench, but there was only 0.8 seconds left — time enough for an airballed 3-point try. The Wildcats looked fresh and feisty from the moment the ball was tipped, perhaps taking advantage of UCF's tired legs following its second-round overtime win over Cincinnati, and roaring to a 35-8 lead over the first 13 minutes of the game. The Knights actually outscored the Wildcats the rest of the first half Thursday, but they still faced a massive 46-30 deficit. profile_summary: UCF enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 10-seed with a 21-11 record — a résumé that reflects genuine Big 12 competitiveness but also real limitations against elite opposition. The Knights finished 9-9 in conference play and went 3-7 against ranked opponents: notable wins include a home upset of then-No. 17 Kansas (81-75), a road demolition of No. 19 BYU (97-84), and a road win at No. 11 Texas Tech, while losses to No. 1 Arizona (twice, including an 81-59 blowout in the Big 12 quarterfinals), No. 9 Iowa State (87-57), and No. 8 Houston (79-55) expose a team that struggles badly when elite defenses take away their perimeter game. Their recent form is concerning — UCF has gone 2-3 in their final five games, losing to West Virginia twice (including a 77-62 road blowout to close the regular season), falling to Oklahoma State in overtime, narrowly edging Cincinnati in a Big 12 Tournament overtime thriller despite going 3-of-24 from three, and then getting throttled by Arizona to end their conference tournament run. The Knights are at their best as a high-volume, high-efficiency three-point shooting team — their 97-84 BYU win featured 14-of-24 shooting from deep, and their Colorado demolition saw 15-of-24 from range — but when that shot isn't falling, as evidenced by the Houston (31% overall), Iowa State, and Big 12 Tournament performances, the offense collapses and opposing teams attack them in the paint with ease, most recently as Arizona outscored them 36-28 in paint points and Houston dominated 42-14. The engine of this team is the Milwaukee transfer tandem of Themus Fulks and Jamichael Stillwell: Fulks delivered a career-high 30 points against Arizona and leads the nation in assists at roughly 6.6 per game, while Stillwell provides elite rebounding and interior toughness with five double-doubles on the season, though his availability against Iowa State — where UCF surrendered 19 turnovers in a 30-point loss — remains worth monitoring. Riley Kugel is their most dangerous perimeter scorer, posting a career-high 26 against Baylor and consistently lighting up mid-major defenses, and Jordan Burks is a reliable secondary option who shot 5-of-7 from deep in a road win at Texas A&M — but this supporting cast vanishes in big moments, as evidenced by the Big 12 Tournament exit. UCF has shown genuine resilience in close games — rallying from 14 down to beat Florida Atlantic, winning on a Fulks buzzer jumper against Cincinnati in January, and surviving Oakland and Quinnipiac in the non-conference slate — but they are also demonstrably collapse-prone, blowing double-digit second-half leads against West Virginia and Arizona State before needing late heroics to survive. At home, UCF is dominant and difficult to beat, but their road and neutral-site body of work is mixed: the BYU and Colorado wins are genuine, but the Iowa State and Houston performances on the road were flat and disengaged, suggesting the team feeds heavily off its home environment. **Verdict:** UCF is a dangerous, boom-or-bust 10-seed whose tournament ceiling is the Round of 32 — they have enough three-point firepower and clutch DNA to pull off a first-round upset against a 7-seed, but their inability to sustain offense when shooting goes cold, their paint vulnerability against physical teams, and their track record of flat performances against elite defenses make a Sweet 16 run an unlikely outcome.

profile_summary: Villanova enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as an 8-seed with a 24-8 record — a résumé built on a strong Big East showing (15-5 in conference) under first-year coach Kevin Willard, but one that carries meaningful caveats against elite competition. Against ranked opponents, the Wildcats struggled, going approximately 0-5 in games against clearly top-tier foes: they were blown out by #2 Michigan 89-61 in December, lost to #2 UConn twice (75-67 in overtime and 73-63), fell to #15 St. John's 89-57 in their most lopsided loss since 1997, and were stunned by #11-seed Georgetown 78-64 in their Big East Tournament opener to end the season. The lone bright spot in high-profile matchups was a competitive overtime loss to UConn in January where they actually led at halftime and pushed the eventual top seed deep into extra time — but the broader pattern is clear: this team has consistently wilted against top-25 caliber opponents, often collapsing in the second half after keeping games competitive through the break. Entering the tournament, Villanova's final five games read: a 19-point win over DePaul, a 13-point win over Xavier, a 14-point loss to Georgetown in the Big East Tournament, and before that a brutal 32-point beatdown at St. John's — a sequence that reflects a team that can dominate weaker competition but is wildly inconsistent when the stakes rise. There is also a significant injury concern: starting forward Matt Hodge suffered a right leg injury in the St. John's blowout, and Willard offered no update post-game; if Hodge is limited or unavailable, Villanova loses one of its most efficient shooters (7-of-9 from the field, three threes in a dominant early-season showing) and a perimeter threat the offense genuinely needs. The team's identity is a multi-scorer half-court attack anchored by Acaden Lewis — a versatile, high-assist lead guard who posted 26 points and six assists at Georgetown, 21 points and four steals in an overtime win at Xavier, and consistently elevates in road and clutch moments — supported by Tyler Perkins (physical, 22-point closer against Marquette), Duke Brennan (a double-double threat inside who scored 21 and grabbed 12 in the Creighton win), and Bryce Lindsay as a volume three-point shooter off the bounce. What makes this team dangerous is their depth, their ability to orchestrate late-game runs (13-0 second-half spurts, 16-0 runs, 11-2 closing bursts appear repeatedly in the summaries), and Devin Askew as a high-efficiency spark plug off the bench who shot 5-for-8 from three in one Providence blowout. Villanova is measurably stronger at home (they were 6-0 at home through early December and rarely lost there in Big East play) and has shown genuine mettle in road wins at Butler, Georgetown, Providence, and Xavier — but the neutral-site and true top-25 environments have consistently exposed their defensive limitations and perimeter shooting fragility (6-for-24 from three in the second UConn loss is emblematic). The team has a scrappy, resilient identity in the mid-range of competition — they rally, they close, and Lewis in particular projects as a tournament-caliber go-to performer — but the ceiling is firmly capped by a troubling inability to sustain intensity for full 40-minute efforts against elite defenses. **Verdict: Villanova's realistic tournament ceiling is the Round of 32.** They are dangerous enough as an 8-seed to steal a first-round win against a potentially over-seeded 9-seed opponent, with Lewis and Perkins capable of carrying them on a good night — but the profile of recurring second-half collapses against top competition, potential Hodge injury uncertainty, and a 0-5 record against ranked opponents makes any run deeper than one game an optimistic stretch.

profile_summary:
Utah State enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 9-seed with a 28-6 record and a Mountain West Championship, but that seeding almost certainly understates their quality — this is a team that went 15-1 out of the gate, won their conference tournament, and beat San Diego State twice in the bracket. Against quality competition, the Aggies showed they can dominate: they dismantled Colorado State 100-58, blew out Air Force 99-62, routed Wyoming 94-62, and beat Boise State twice by comfortable margins. However, losses to UNLV (twice), Nevada, and San Diego State in the regular season reveal a team that can be beaten when opponents bring physicality and second-half execution — most notably, UNLV torched them 92-65 in early March, outscoring Utah State by 19 in the second half in a genuine blowout. Their five-game closing stretch tells a layered story: they dropped a regular-season finale to San Diego State 89-72, bounced back to beat New Mexico 94-90 in a high-scoring game, then rolled through the Mountain West Tournament — defeating UNLV 80-60, Nevada 79-66, and San Diego State 73-62 to claim the title. That conference tournament run is the most encouraging recent evidence, showing a team that has tightened up precisely when it matters most.

Stylistically, Utah State is a high-octane offensive team capable of both wire-to-wire blowouts and grinding out close wins, but they are not immune to second-half collapses — UNLV exposed them twice, and Nevada erased a six-point halftime lead before Utah State recovered in the regular season. The engine of this team is Mason Falslev, the Mountain West Player of the Year, who delivered 16 points and six assists in the conference championship and has been the Aggies' most complete player all season. MJ Collins Jr. is the explosive co-star — he erupted for a tournament-record 40 points against Davidson in November and scored 20 in the conference title game, draining clutch back-to-back 3-pointers in the decisive run — but his shot can go cold (3-of-14 against San Diego State in January). Drake Allen provides versatile, multi-category production, and Kolby King and Karson Templin offer reliable bench scoring when the starters are held in check. Utah State has also shown it performs well on neutral sites — winning the Charleston Classic in November and capturing the Mountain West Tournament in Las Vegas — suggesting the NCAA Tournament setting should not be a psychological obstacle. This is not a team that wilts; they are composed, experienced, and capable of beating anyone in the bracket on a given night. **Realistic ceiling: Elite Eight.** Utah State is dangerous enough to beat a higher seed in the first two rounds and has the star power and neutral-site pedigree to advance, but their pattern of second-half vulnerability against elite, physical opposition raises enough doubt to project a likely ceiling short of the Final Four.

The Tigers began to heat up in the second half and closed the gap to 12 with 2 1/2 minutes remaining, but Cameron Boozer hit a long 3 from the top of the key to thwart the rally. Up next Clemson: Awaits NCAA Tournament seed. Duke: The Blue Devils beat Virginia 77-51 at Cameron Indoor Stadium on Feb. 28. They will look for another dominant performance in the championship game. ------ Get poll alerts and updates on the AP Top 25 throughout the season. Sign up here. AP college basketball: and
profile_summary: Clemson enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as an 8-seed with a 24-10 record — a résumé built on a strong ACC campaign that gradually unraveled down the stretch. The Tigers were genuinely elite in conference play at one point, reaching 10-1 in the ACC and cracking the AP Top 25, but a brutal late-season slide exposed real vulnerability: Clemson lost five of their final seven regular-season games, including home defeats to Virginia Tech, Florida State, and a blowout at Wake Forest where they trailed by 20 in the first half. Against ranked opponents, the Tigers went approximately 4-8, with notable wins over #24 SMU, #24 Louisville, and a dramatic overtime Charleston Classic victory over Georgia, but they were consistently handled by elite competition — losing to #10 BYU (blowing a 22-point halftime lead), #12 Alabama, and #4 Duke twice, including a 67-54 dismantling at Cameron Indoor where Clemson shot 35% and made just 5 of 15 layups. In the ACC Tournament, Clemson showed genuine fight — rallying past North Carolina 80-79 after nearly surrendering an 18-point lead before getting outclassed by Duke 73-61, with the Blue Devils closing the first half on a 26-6 run. The most significant injury concern is forward Carter Welling, who suffered a torn ACL in the ACC Tournament opener and is lost for the season — a massive blow, as Welling was a cornerstone of Clemson's frontcourt identity, providing rebounding, interior finishing, and timely clutch moments, including 11 fourth-quarter points to beat West Virginia in November. The offense runs through multiple contributors — Jestin Porter, RJ Godfrey, Nick Davidson, and Ace Buckner can all take over on a given night — but the supporting cast is maddeningly inconsistent, and Clemson has demonstrated a troubling tendency to collapse from large leads (the BYU and North Carolina ACC Tournament games both featured near-catastrophic meltdowns). Locationally, the Tigers were a fortress at home early (7-0 to start) but lost that edge late, while their 14-game ACC road win streak is genuinely impressive — though it ended with back-to-back losses at Virginia Tech and Duke. The team projects as scrappy and resilient in close games but alarmingly prone to second-half collapses against high-octane opponents, and without Welling patrolling the frontcourt, their interior toughness takes a significant hit entering the bracket. **Verdict: Clemson is a dangerous first-round opponent capable of an upset, but their realistic ceiling is the Round of 32 — the four-game losing streak, Welling's absence, and a pattern of second-half disintegration against elite teams make a Sweet 16 run unlikely.**

profile_summary:
Iowa enters the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 9-seed with a 21-12 record — a résumé that reflects a program that genuinely overachieved under first-year coach Ben McCollum, who brought six players from Drake including star guard Bennett Stirtz, but one that also reveals significant limitations against elite competition. Against ranked opponents this season, the Hawkeyes went 1-9: their lone ranked win came over No. 9 Nebraska at home on February 18, a 57-52 grind-it-out victory that sent Hawkeyes fans storming the court, while losses came against No. 7 Michigan State, No. 4 Iowa State, No. 16 Illinois, No. 5 Purdue (twice), No. 13 Purdue, No. 24 Wisconsin, No. 9 Nebraska (twice), and No. 3 Michigan — a brutal body of evidence suggesting this team consistently falls short when the competition crests into the top tier. Their final five games paint a picture of a team that is inconsistent and grinding rather than surging: a home win over Nebraska (57-52), a road loss at Wisconsin (71-84), a home win over Ohio State (74-57), a road loss at Penn State (69-71) to a team that finished 3-15 in Big Ten play, and a road loss at Nebraska in overtime (75-84) — a 2-3 stretch that includes a damaging defeat to one of the Big Ten's worst conference teams and a regulation collapse that required a miraculous Kael Combs three-pointer just to force OT. In the Big Ten Tournament, Iowa showed genuine fight — beating Maryland decisively behind a 21-0 run fueled by Cooper Koch's career-high 19 points and Stirtz's eight assists — before falling to Ohio State 72-69 in a game they led by only six at halftime and ultimately lost on a missed Folgueiras free throw and a desperation heave at the buzzer, a collapse-prone ending that has become something of a calling card.

Stirtz is unquestionably the engine of this offense and one of the more dangerous scorers in the country entering March — he posted a career-high 36 points against Northwestern, followed it with 32 in consecutive weeks, and is averaging north of 20 points over his last ten games with the ability to get hot from three and draw fouls at will — but Iowa's supporting cast is maddeningly inconsistent, and the team's identity shifts week to week in ways that raise tournament concerns. When Tavion Banks (career-high 26 at Indiana) and Folgueiras (season-high 20 against Ohio State) are engaged, this offense has legitimate punch and real depth; when they go cold, as happened in the back-to-back losses to Maryland and Purdue in mid-February, the Hawkeyes fall apart shooting-wise and struggle to score off turnovers. Stylistically, Iowa is a team that builds large first-half leads and then spends the second half defending them, often barely — the UCLA game (led by 20, nearly blew it), the USC game (led by 17, nearly blew it), and the Nebraska OT loss all reflect a team prone to second-half fade. Location matters: Iowa is undefeated at home in non-conference play and dominant in Iowa City through much of the season, but they are a concerning 5-8 away from home and on neutral sites, which is exactly where the NCAA Tournament is played. The Hawkeyes carry a scrappy, resilient energy and McCollum has clearly built genuine culture fast — the court-storming Nebraska win and the Big Ten Tournament comeback over Maryland show they can rise to moments — but a 1-9 record against ranked teams, a penchant for second-half collapses, and road/neutral-site struggles make a deep run implausible. **Ceiling: First-round exit to second-round upset pick.** Stirtz's star power makes Iowa dangerous enough to steal one game as a 9-seed, potentially pulling off an upset over an 8-seed in the first round if his shot is falling, but this team has shown no ability to beat elite competition consistently and is too prone to second-half fade and neutral-site struggles to advance past the second round.
